Inferior MI from RCA occlusion can cause necrosis and rupture of this papillary muscle 2-7 days post-MI, resulting in acute severe MR with cardiogenic shock. This is a surgical emergency requiring urgent mitral valve repair or replacement.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The anterolateral papillary muscle has dual blood supply from the LAD (diagonal branches) and LCx (obtuse marginal branches), making it much less susceptible to complete rupture from a single coronary occlusion.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Rheumatic MR develops chronically over years from leaflet thickening and retraction, not acutely after MI. There is no history suggesting rheumatic disease.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Mitral annular calcification causes chronic MR by restricting leaflet motion. It does not cause acute flail leaflet or sudden hemodynamic deterioration.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While endocarditis can cause acute MR from leaflet destruction, this patient has a clear temporal relationship to MI (3 days post-event) without systemic signs of infection.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman presents with palpitations and atypical chest pain. She is tall and thin with a low BMI. On auscultation, there is a mid-systolic click followed by a late systolic murmur at the apex. When she stands up from a squatting position, which of the following changes would be expected?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The click and murmur move later in systole

choices.label: B
choices.text: Both the click and murmur disappear entirely

choices.label: C
choices.text: The click disappears but the murmur remains unchanged

choices.label: D
choices.text: The click and murmur move earlier in systole

explanation.summary: Standing from squatting decreases venous return and LV volume. In a smaller ventricle, the redundant mitral valve leaflets reach their prolapse point earlier in systole, causing the click and murmur to move earlier.
explanation.whyCorrect: MVP occurs when the mitral leaflets prolapse past the annular plane as the LV contracts. With decreased preload (standing, Valsalva), the LV is smaller at the onset of systole, so the redundant leaflets reach their maximum excursion and prolapse earlier. This moves the click closer to S1 and lengthens the duration of the murmur. Conversely, increased preload (squatting) delays prolapse, moving the click toward S2.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Moving the click later would occur with maneuvers that increase LV volume (squatting, leg elevation), not with standing, which decreases preload.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Standing would not eliminate the findings — it would accentuate them by making the click earlier and the murmur longer.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The click is an integral part of MVP auscultation and does not disappear independently of the murmur. Both are affected similarly by changes in LV volume.
whyWrongByChoice.E: MVP produces a systolic murmur from mitral regurgitation. It does not convert to a diastolic murmur with positional changes.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with chronic severe primary mitral regurgitation from myxomatous degeneration is asymptomatic. Echocardiography shows LVEF of 62%, LV end-systolic diameter of 38 mm, left atrial volume index of 55 mL/m², and pulmonary artery systolic pressure of 42 mmHg. Exercise testing shows good functional capacity without symptoms. Which of the following is the most appropriate management?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Medical management with ACE inhibitor and annual follow-up

choices.label: B
choices.text: Add spironolactone and beta-blocker for afterload reduction

choices.label: E
choices.text: Refer for mitral valve surgery given elevated LA volume and PA pressure

explanation.summary: This asymptomatic patient with severe primary MR has LA dilation (LAVI >40 mL/m²) and elevated PASP (>40 mmHg at rest, confirmed by echo). These are class IIa triggers for surgical intervention, especially if repair likelihood is >95% at an experienced center.
explanation.whyCorrect: ACC/AHA guidelines support mitral valve surgery (class IIa) for asymptomatic severe primary MR when there are signs of hemodynamic consequence: PASP >50 mmHg, new-onset AF, or progressive LV dilation/dysfunction. While LVEF and LVESD are not at traditional thresholds, the elevated PASP and significant LA dilation indicate the regurgitation is having hemodynamic effects. When surgical repair likelihood is high, early intervention prevents irreversible atrial and ventricular remodeling.

whyWrongByChoice.A: ACE inhibitors have no proven benefit in chronic primary MR with normal LV function. Watchful waiting risks progressive atrial and ventricular remodeling beyond the point of reversibility.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Vasodilator therapy does not reduce regurgitant volume in primary MR with normal LV function and is not guideline-recommended as a strategy to delay surgery.
whyWrongByChoice.C: While surveillance is an option for asymptomatic patients without hemodynamic triggers, this patient has elevated PASP and significant LA dilation, which favor early surgical referral.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Transcatheter MV replacement is reserved for patients at high/prohibitive surgical risk. This patient appears to be a good surgical candidate, and repair of myxomatous disease has excellent outcomes.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with ischemic cardiomyopathy (LVEF 28%) and severe functional mitral regurgitation is evaluated. The MR jet is central, and the leaflets are structurally normal but tethered by displaced papillary muscles. He has NYHA class III symptoms despite optimal medical therapy including sacubitril-valsartan, carvedilol, spironolactone, and an ICD. Which of the following is the most appropriate intervention for his MR?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Surgical mitral valve repair with ring annuloplasty

choices.label: C
choices.text: Intensify diuretic therapy without valve intervention

explanation.summary: For severe secondary (functional) MR in patients with reduced LVEF who remain symptomatic on optimal GDMT, transcatheter edge-to-edge repair (TEER/MitraClip) is a class IIa recommendation based on the COAPT trial.
explanation.whyCorrect: The COAPT trial demonstrated that transcatheter edge-to-edge repair significantly reduced HF hospitalizations and mortality in patients with HFrEF and moderate-to-severe or severe secondary MR despite optimal medical therapy. Key COAPT criteria include LVEF 20-50%, LVESD ≤70 mm, and MR that is disproportionate to the degree of LV dilation. This patient meets these criteria.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Surgical MVR for secondary MR has not shown survival benefit in randomized trials and carries higher operative risk in patients with severely reduced LVEF.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Surgical repair with ring annuloplasty for secondary MR has high recurrence rates (up to 60% at 2 years) because the underlying ventricular pathology persists. The CTSN trial showed no survival advantage of repair over replacement.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The patient is already on optimal medical therapy with persistent severe symptoms. Intensifying diuretics without addressing the MR is unlikely to provide meaningful improvement.
whyWrongByChoice.D: CRT may improve functional MR by improving LV synchrony and reducing tethering, but it is indicated for patients with LBBB and QRS >150 ms. It would not be sufficient as the sole intervention if MR remains severe on GDMT.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old woman with mitral valve prolapse undergoes echocardiography showing bileaflet prolapse with severe MR. The posterior leaflet shows a flail P2 segment. She is referred for surgery. Which of the following procedures is preferred over mitral valve replacement in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Mitral valve repair with triangular resection and ring annuloplasty

choices.label: C
choices.text: Mechanical mitral valve replacement with warfarin

explanation.summary: Mitral valve repair is strongly preferred over replacement for degenerative MR, especially for posterior leaflet pathology (P2 flail). Repair preserves native valve tissue, avoids anticoagulation, has lower operative mortality, and provides superior long-term outcomes.
explanation.whyCorrect: Posterior leaflet pathology (P2 segment) is the most amenable to surgical repair, with success rates >95% at experienced centers. Repair techniques include triangular or quadrangular resection of the prolapsing segment, chordal transfer or artificial chordae (ePTFE), and annuloplasty ring placement. Repair preserves the subvalvular apparatus, maintaining LV geometry and function.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Bioprosthetic MVR avoids lifelong anticoagulation but has inferior durability and hemodynamics compared to repair. It is reserved for patients whose valves cannot be repaired.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Mechanical MVR requires lifelong warfarin anticoagulation with bleeding risk. It is reserved for younger patients who cannot have repair and have high valve failure risk with bioprostheses.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Transcatheter MVR is investigational and reserved for high-risk patients unsuitable for surgery. A 50-year-old surgical candidate should receive standard surgical repair.
whyWrongByChoice.E: PMBC is used for mitral stenosis with commissural fusion, not for mitral regurgitation from leaflet prolapse.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with chronic severe mitral regurgitation presents with new-onset atrial fibrillation and worsening dyspnea (NYHA class II). Echocardiography shows LVEF of 55%, LVESD of 42 mm, and severe eccentric MR jet directed posteriorly. Which of the following echocardiographic findings in chronic MR is the most reliable indicator that LV systolic function is already impaired despite the 'normal' EF?

choices.label: D
choices.text: LVEF of 55% in the setting of severe volume overload

explanation.summary: In chronic severe MR, LVEF overestimates true systolic function because part of the stroke volume is ejected into the low-pressure LA (reducing effective afterload). An EF of 55% in this setting likely indicates early contractile impairment — a normal MR ventricle should have an EF of 65-70%.
explanation.whyCorrect: The LV in chronic MR ejects into two outlets: the aorta (high pressure) and the LA (low pressure). This 'unloading' effect makes the LVEF appear higher than it would be if all ejection were forward. Guidelines recognize this by using an LVEF threshold of <60% (not <50%) as an indication for surgery in severe MR. An EF of 55% in severe MR represents meaningful contractile dysfunction.

whyWrongByChoice.A: LV end-diastolic dilation reflects chronic volume overload and remodeling but does not specifically indicate LV systolic dysfunction. LVESD is a better marker of systolic function.
whyWrongByChoice.B: New AF is an important prognostic marker and a trigger for surgical intervention in severe MR, but it reflects atrial remodeling rather than directly indicating LV systolic impairment.
whyWrongByChoice.C: An eccentric jet pattern indicates the mechanism of MR (e.g., flail leaflet) but does not inform about LV systolic function.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Jet direction indicates which leaflet is abnormal (posterior jet = anterior leaflet pathology and vice versa) but does not assess LV function.

questions.stem: A 27-year-old woman with Marfan syndrome presents with an episode of transient visual loss. Examination reveals a mid-systolic click and late systolic murmur. TEE shows MVP with a small mobile filamentous strand attached to the atrial side of the mitral valve. Brain MRI shows a small acute ischemic infarct. Which of the following is the most likely source of her cerebral embolism?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Left atrial thrombus from atrial fibrillation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Fibrin strand on the prolapsing mitral valve

choices.label: D
choices.text: Paradoxical embolism through a patent foramen ovale

explanation.summary: MVP is associated with fibrin strands (fibrinous deposits) on the atrial surface of prolapsing leaflets, which can serve as a nidus for thrombus formation and embolization. These strands are a recognized cause of cryptogenic stroke in young patients with MVP.
explanation.whyCorrect: The myxomatous mitral valve in MVP can develop fibrin deposits and small thrombi on the atrial surface where endothelial damage occurs from leaflet contact and jet lesions. The mobile filamentous strand described on TEE is consistent with this finding. In a young patient with MVP and no other stroke risk factors, this is the most likely embolic source.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Carotid atherosclerosis is extremely unlikely in a 27-year-old without cardiovascular risk factors. Marfan syndrome does not predispose to atherosclerosis.
whyWrongByChoice.B: LA thrombus from AF is unlikely in a 27-year-old. There is no mention of AF, and the TEE finding specifically identifies a valvular strand rather than an atrial thrombus.
whyWrongByChoice.D: PFO with paradoxical embolism is a consideration in cryptogenic stroke, but the TEE finding of a mobile strand on the mitral valve provides a more direct embolic source explanation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: IE would present with fever, positive blood cultures, and vegetations with different echocardiographic characteristics (irregular, echogenic masses). The filamentous strand and absence of systemic infection make IE unlikely.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man develops acute severe mitral regurgitation during an ST-elevation myocardial infarction involving the inferior wall. He is in cardiogenic shock with a blood pressure of 80/60 mmHg and a new holosystolic murmur. While awaiting emergent surgery, which of the following temporizing measures is most appropriate?

explanation.summary: IABP counterpulsation is the ideal temporizing measure for acute severe MR with cardiogenic shock. It reduces afterload (systolic balloon deflation), augments coronary perfusion (diastolic balloon inflation), and decreases the regurgitant fraction, improving forward cardiac output.
explanation.whyCorrect: The IABP deflates during systole, reducing aortic pressure and LV afterload, which decreases the pressure gradient driving MR and promotes forward flow. It inflates during diastole, augmenting coronary perfusion — critical in the setting of acute MI. Unlike IABP in aortic stenosis or AR (where it is contraindicated), IABP is highly effective in acute MR.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Phenylephrine is a pure alpha-1 agonist that increases SVR and afterload, which would worsen the regurgitant fraction by increasing the systolic pressure gradient across the mitral valve.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Large volume fluid resuscitation would increase preload, worsening pulmonary congestion and LV distension without addressing the regurgitant lesion. This patient needs afterload reduction, not volume.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Esmolol (beta-blocker) would decrease heart rate and contractility, potentially worsening cardiogenic shock. Negative inotropy is dangerous in this hemodynamically unstable patient.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Oral hydralazine has slow onset, unpredictable absorption in shock (reduced gut perfusion), and cannot be titrated. IV vasodilators or mechanical support are more appropriate in this acute setting.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man with mitral valve prolapse undergoes echocardiography that shows myxomatous thickening of both mitral leaflets (>5 mm) with bileaflet prolapse and mild MR. He has no symptoms and exercises regularly. His father had sudden cardiac death at age 45. Which of the following findings would most increase his risk of adverse outcomes?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Mitral annular disjunction with ventricular arrhythmias on Holter

choices.label: B
choices.text: Presence of a mid-systolic click on auscultation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Mild mitral regurgitation by color Doppler

choices.label: E
choices.text: Asymptomatic status with normal exercise capacity

explanation.summary: Mitral annular disjunction (MAD) — separation between the LA wall and the LV myocardium at the mitral annulus — combined with ventricular arrhythmias identifies a high-risk subset of MVP patients prone to sudden cardiac death, especially with a family history of SCD.
explanation.whyCorrect: MAD creates abnormal mechanical stress on the papillary muscles and adjacent myocardium, promoting fibrosis and providing a substrate for ventricular arrhythmias. When combined with complex ventricular ectopy (PVCs, NSVT on Holter), bileaflet MVP, myxomatous thickening, and a family history of SCD, this constitutes 'arrhythmic MVP' — a recognized high-risk phenotype that may warrant ICD consideration.

whyWrongByChoice.B: A mid-systolic click is a common auscultatory finding in MVP and does not independently predict adverse outcomes or sudden death.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Mild MR in MVP is common and has an excellent prognosis. It is the degree of MR progression and LV remodeling that determines outcomes.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Bileaflet prolapse is one component of the high-risk phenotype, but without MAD or arrhythmias, it does not independently confer the same degree of risk.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Asymptomatic status and normal exercise capacity are reassuring features that do not increase risk.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman with chronic severe primary MR undergoes mitral valve repair. Six months postoperatively, her LVEF has decreased from a preoperative value of 65% to 50%. She feels well and is more active than before surgery. Which of the following best explains the postoperative decline in LVEF?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Surgical damage to the circumflex coronary artery

choices.label: B
choices.text: New ischemic cardiomyopathy from graft failure

choices.label: C
choices.text: Prosthetic valve dysfunction with paravalvular leak

choices.label: E
choices.text: Elimination of the low-impedance pathway into the LA, unmasking true LV function

explanation.summary: After correction of severe MR, the LV no longer ejects into the low-pressure LA. All ejection must now occur against systemic afterload, increasing effective afterload and unmasking the true (lower) contractile function. A modest decline in LVEF is expected and common.
explanation.whyCorrect: Preoperatively, the incompetent mitral valve provided a 'pop-off' into the low-pressure LA during systole, artificially inflating the measured LVEF. After repair, 100% of stroke volume must be ejected into the aorta against full systemic afterload. The resulting drop in EF reflects the true contractile state and is an expected, well-described phenomenon — not a surgical complication.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Circumflex artery injury is a rare surgical complication that would cause regional wall motion abnormalities and ischemic symptoms, not a global EF decline in an asymptomatic patient who is more active.
whyWrongByChoice.B: There is no mention of CABG or graft failure. The patient underwent isolated mitral valve repair and is clinically improved.
whyWrongByChoice.C: This patient underwent valve repair, not replacement. Paravalvular leak is a complication of prosthetic valves. Recurrent MR after repair would maintain or increase EF, not decrease it.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Constrictive pericarditis would present with signs of right heart failure, pericardial thickening, and hemodynamic impairment, not isolated EF decline with improved functional status.

questions.stem: A 44-year-old woman presents with a murmur. On auscultation, there is a holosystolic murmur at the apex that radiates to the axilla. Handgrip exercise intensifies the murmur. The murmur does not change with inspiration. Which of the following conditions is most consistent with these findings?

explanation.summary: A holosystolic murmur at the apex radiating to the axilla that increases with handgrip (which increases afterload) and does not vary with respiration is classic for mitral regurgitation.
explanation.whyCorrect: MR produces a holosystolic murmur at the apex radiating to the axilla (for posterior leaflet pathology) or the base (for anterior leaflet pathology). Handgrip increases systemic vascular resistance (afterload), which increases the pressure gradient driving blood from LV to LA, worsening MR and intensifying the murmur. As a left-sided murmur, it does not change with inspiration.

whyWrongByChoice.A: AS produces a crescendo-decrescendo (not holosystolic) systolic murmur at the right upper sternal border. Handgrip typically decreases the AS murmur by reducing LV stroke volume.
whyWrongByChoice.B: HOCM produces a crescendo-decrescendo murmur at the LLSB that decreases with handgrip (increased afterload reduces the dynamic obstruction). This is the opposite of what is described.
whyWrongByChoice.D: TR produces a holosystolic murmur at the left lower sternal border (not apex to axilla) that increases with inspiration (Carvallo sign). This murmur does not change with inspiration.
whyWrongByChoice.E: VSD produces a holosystolic murmur at the left sternal border (not apex to axilla). While it increases with handgrip, the location and radiation pattern do not match.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with severe ischemic MR and LVEF of 30% undergoes coronary angiography showing triple-vessel disease amenable to CABG. His MR is secondary to LV dilation with tethered but structurally normal leaflets. Which of the following is the most appropriate surgical strategy regarding the mitral valve?

choices.label: A
choices.text: CABG alone without addressing the mitral valve

choices.label: B
choices.text: CABG with concurrent mitral valve replacement using a mechanical prosthesis

choices.label: C
choices.text: CABG with concurrent mitral valve repair (restrictive annuloplasty)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Mitral valve replacement alone without CABG

choices.label: E
choices.text: CABG with concurrent transcatheter edge-to-edge repair

explanation.summary: For severe secondary (ischemic) MR in patients undergoing CABG, concurrent mitral repair with restrictive annuloplasty is recommended. Revascularization alone may not resolve the MR, and adding annuloplasty addresses the dilated annulus contributing to leaflet malcoaptation.
explanation.whyCorrect: In severe secondary MR with indication for CABG, adding mitral repair (undersized rigid annuloplasty ring) is reasonable because revascularization alone incompletely addresses the MR in many patients. The CTSN moderate ischemic MR trial showed that adding repair to CABG reduced MR severity more effectively than CABG alone, though survival was similar at 2 years.

whyWrongByChoice.A: CABG alone may not resolve severe secondary MR because the MR results from LV remodeling and annular dilation, not purely from ischemia. While mild-to-moderate ischemic MR may improve with revascularization, severe MR often persists.
whyWrongByChoice.B: MVR with a mechanical prosthesis requires lifelong anticoagulation and has higher operative mortality than repair. In secondary MR, repair with annuloplasty is preferred when feasible.
whyWrongByChoice.D: MVR alone without CABG does not address the underlying coronary artery disease, which drives the ischemic cardiomyopathy. Revascularization is essential for improving LV function.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Transcatheter edge-to-edge repair is not typically performed concurrently with open-heart CABG. It is a percutaneous procedure indicated for patients who are not surgical candidates.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman with a history of intravenous drug use presents with fever, cough, and pleuritic chest pain for 1 week. Her temperature is 39.2°C, blood pressure is 110/70 mmHg, and heart rate is 105 bpm. Examination reveals a holosystolic murmur at the left lower sternal border that increases with inspiration, distended neck veins with prominent 'cv' waves, and a pulsatile liver. Chest CT shows multiple bilateral peripheral nodular infiltrates with cavitation. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: E
choices.text: Infective endocarditis of the tricuspid valve with septic pulmonary emboli

explanation.summary: IV drug use, fever, a new TR murmur (holosystolic at LLSB, increases with inspiration), and bilateral cavitating pulmonary nodules constitute the classic triad of right-sided infective endocarditis with septic pulmonary emboli.
explanation.whyCorrect: IVDU patients are at high risk for tricuspid valve endocarditis (Staphylococcus aureus is the most common organism). Vegetations on the tricuspid valve embolize to the pulmonary vasculature, producing multiple peripheral cavitating nodules (septic emboli). The murmur of TR with Carvallo sign (inspiratory increase), JVD with cv waves, and pulsatile hepatomegaly all point to tricuspid valve involvement.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Community-acquired pneumonia typically presents with lobar consolidation, not bilateral peripheral cavitating nodules. Empyema is a unilateral pleural process. The cardiac findings are unexplained by pneumonia.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Miliary TB produces diffuse tiny (1-3 mm) nodules throughout both lungs, not peripheral cavitating nodules. It does not explain the new TR murmur.
whyWrongByChoice.D: GPA can cause cavitating pulmonary nodules, but it typically presents with upper airway involvement (sinusitis, epistaxis) and glomerulonephritis, not a new cardiac murmur in an IV drug user.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Metastatic lung cancer would be unusual in a 45-year-old and would not cause fever, acute onset, or a new TR murmur.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old woman with long-standing rheumatic mitral stenosis and chronic atrial fibrillation presents with worsening peripheral edema, ascites, and fatigue. On examination, she has jugular venous distension with a slow 'y' descent, a diastolic rumble at the left lower sternal border that increases with inspiration, and hepatomegaly. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hepatic cirrhosis from chronic right heart failure

explanation.summary: A diastolic rumble at the LLSB that increases with inspiration in a patient with rheumatic mitral stenosis indicates concurrent rheumatic tricuspid stenosis. The slow y descent (impaired RV filling) and right-sided congestion are hallmarks of TS.
explanation.whyCorrect: Rheumatic heart disease can affect multiple valves. TS is almost always rheumatic in etiology and is nearly always associated with MS. The diastolic rumble at the LLSB (vs. apex in MS) with inspiratory augmentation localizes it to the right side. The slow y descent reflects impaired passive filling of the RV across the stenotic tricuspid valve. TS can mask MS symptoms by limiting flow to the left heart.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Constrictive pericarditis produces a pericardial knock, rapid y descent (not slow), and Kussmaul sign. The diastolic rumble with inspiratory increase is not a feature.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Budd-Chiari syndrome causes hepatic vein thrombosis with hepatomegaly and ascites but does not cause cardiac murmurs or JVD with abnormal waveforms.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Hepatic cirrhosis from chronic congestion (cardiac cirrhosis) may contribute to the hepatomegaly and ascites, but it does not explain the new diastolic murmur at the LLSB.
whyWrongByChoice.E: IVC obstruction causes lower extremity edema and hepatomegaly but would not produce a diastolic murmur at the LLSB or JVD with characteristic waveform changes.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman with severe functional tricuspid regurgitation secondary to left heart disease undergoes echocardiography. The tricuspid annulus diameter is 48 mm (normal <35 mm). She has been treated with diuretics for 6 months but continues to have severe peripheral edema and recurrent ascites requiring paracentesis. The left-sided valve disease has been corrected with prior mitral valve replacement. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increase diuretic doses and add metolazone

choices.label: D
choices.text: Refer for isolated tricuspid valve surgery

explanation.summary: Severe symptomatic TR with significant annular dilation (>40 mm) refractory to medical therapy, particularly after correction of the primary left-sided lesion, is an indication for isolated tricuspid valve surgery (repair or replacement).
explanation.whyCorrect: When TR persists or worsens after correction of the underlying left heart disease, isolated tricuspid valve surgery is indicated for symptomatic severe TR. The markedly dilated annulus (48 mm) suggests the TR will not improve with further medical therapy alone. Early referral is important because progressive RV dysfunction and hepatic congestion worsen surgical outcomes.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Escalating diuretics provides temporary symptom relief but does not address the mechanical valve problem. The patient has already failed a reasonable course of medical therapy.
whyWrongByChoice.B: While cardiac cirrhosis can develop from chronic TR, liver transplantation is not appropriate when the underlying cardiac cause can be treated surgically.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Pulmonary vasodilators are indicated for pulmonary arterial hypertension (Group 1), not for secondary TR from left heart disease where the primary lesion has already been corrected.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Palliative care referral is premature when there is a correctable surgical lesion. Tricuspid valve surgery can significantly improve quality of life and symptoms.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman undergoing right heart catheterization for evaluation of pulmonary hypertension has a mean right atrial pressure of 18 mmHg. The RA pressure tracing shows a prominent 'v' wave that equals the RV systolic pressure. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: A giant 'v' wave in the RA pressure tracing that approaches or equals RV systolic pressure indicates severe tricuspid regurgitation. The v wave represents systolic reflux of blood back into the RA through the incompetent tricuspid valve.
explanation.whyCorrect: In severe TR, the RV ejects blood backward into the RA during systole, creating a large systolic wave (giant v wave or 'cv' wave, as the c and v waves merge). When the v wave amplitude equals the RV systolic pressure, it indicates free TR with essentially no barrier between the RV and RA during systole.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Tricuspid stenosis produces an elevated mean RA pressure with a prominent 'a' wave (from atrial contraction against the stenotic valve) and a slow y descent, not a giant v wave.
whyWrongByChoice.C: RA myxoma can cause right-sided obstruction with elevated RA pressure but would produce findings similar to TS (prominent a wave) and would not generate a systolic v wave equaling RV pressure.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Cardiac tamponade shows equalization of diastolic pressures (RA = RV diastolic = PCWP) with x descent prominence and blunted y descent, not giant v waves.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Constrictive pericarditis shows equalized diastolic pressures with a prominent y descent ('dip-and-plateau' or 'square root sign' in RV tracing), not giant v waves.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man who underwent mitral valve replacement 5 years ago now presents with progressive dyspnea, peripheral edema, and ascites. Echocardiography shows a well-functioning mitral prosthesis, moderate-to-severe functional TR with tethering of the tricuspid leaflets, and a dilated RV with reduced systolic function. The tricuspid annulus is 44 mm. At the time of his initial surgery, the tricuspid valve appeared normal. Which of the following would have been the most appropriate intraoperative strategy to prevent his current condition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Prophylactic tricuspid annuloplasty at the time of MVR

choices.label: B
choices.text: Tricuspid valve replacement at the time of MVR

choices.label: C
choices.text: Postoperative bosentan therapy to prevent PH

choices.label: D
choices.text: Left atrial appendage ligation to prevent AF

explanation.summary: Current guidelines recommend prophylactic tricuspid annuloplasty at the time of left-sided valve surgery when the tricuspid annulus is dilated (>40 mm) or there is at least mild TR, even without severe TR, to prevent progressive functional TR.
explanation.whyCorrect: Late development of significant TR after left-sided valve surgery is common and carries poor prognosis. Redo isolated tricuspid surgery has higher mortality than concomitant repair at index operation. ACC/AHA guidelines give a class IIa recommendation for tricuspid annuloplasty at the time of left-sided surgery when the TA is dilated (>40 mm) or when there is mild-or-greater TR, as it prevents progressive annular dilation.

whyWrongByChoice.B: TV replacement would be excessive for a structurally normal valve with mild or no TR. Annuloplasty preserves the native valve while preventing progressive dilation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Bosentan (endothelin receptor antagonist) is for PAH (Group 1), not for prevention of secondary TR. It does not prevent tricuspid annular dilation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: LAA ligation may reduce stroke risk in AF but does not prevent TR development. AF contributes to TR through RA dilation, but addressing the annulus directly is the appropriate preventive measure.
whyWrongByChoice.E: This case illustrates why watchful waiting is insufficient. Progressive TR requiring reoperative surgery could have been prevented with prophylactic annuloplasty.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman with Ebstein anomaly presents for evaluation. Echocardiography shows apical displacement of the septal and posterior tricuspid valve leaflets by 22 mm with severe TR. There is a large 'atrialized' portion of the right ventricle. She is in normal sinus rhythm and has an oxygen saturation of 92% on room air. Which of the following congenital findings is most commonly associated with this condition?

choices.label: E
choices.text: Patent foramen ovale or atrial septal defect

explanation.summary: Ebstein anomaly is associated with PFO or ASD in approximately 50-80% of cases. The interatrial communication allows right-to-left shunting when RA pressure is elevated, explaining the mild desaturation (92%).
explanation.whyCorrect: The atrialized portion of the RV in Ebstein anomaly creates a functionally small RV, elevating RA pressure. An associated PFO or ASD permits right-to-left shunting, causing systemic desaturation and risk of paradoxical embolism. The O2 sat of 92% in this patient suggests a small right-to-left shunt through such a communication. Ebstein is also associated with accessory pathways (WPW syndrome) in ~20% of cases.

whyWrongByChoice.A: VSD is not a common association with Ebstein anomaly. VSDs are more commonly associated with tetralogy of Fallot, Down syndrome, and other congenital cardiac conditions.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Congenitally corrected transposition (L-TGA) can be associated with an Ebstein-like malformation of the systemic (morphologic tricuspid) AV valve, but standard TGA is not a typical association.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Coarctation of the aorta is associated with bicuspid aortic valve and Turner syndrome, not Ebstein anomaly.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Bicuspid aortic valve is the most common congenital cardiac anomaly overall but is not specifically associated with Ebstein anomaly.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old man with severe tricuspid regurgitation from prior endocarditis has undergone tricuspid valve replacement with a bioprosthetic valve. Three years later, he presents with recurrent right heart failure symptoms. Echocardiography shows structural degeneration of the bioprosthetic tricuspid valve with severe stenosis and regurgitation. Which of the following is a recognized advantage of transcatheter tricuspid valve-in-valve implantation over redo surgical replacement in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Superior long-term durability of the transcatheter valve

choices.label: B
choices.text: Better hemodynamic profile than the original bioprosthesis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Elimination of the need for anticoagulation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Lower procedural mortality compared to redo open-heart surgery

choices.label: E
choices.text: Ability to treat concurrent left-sided valve disease

explanation.summary: Redo tricuspid valve surgery carries significantly higher mortality (up to 10-25%) compared to transcatheter valve-in-valve implantation. The less invasive transcatheter approach avoids repeat sternotomy and cardiopulmonary bypass, reducing procedural risk.
explanation.whyCorrect: Reoperative tricuspid valve surgery is one of the highest-risk cardiac surgical procedures due to adhesions, risk of RV injury during sternal re-entry, and the fragile hemodynamic state of patients with RV failure. Transcatheter valve-in-valve implantation within the degenerated bioprosthesis avoids these risks and has demonstrated lower procedural mortality in registries.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Long-term durability data for transcatheter valves in the tricuspid position are still limited. There is no evidence that they outlast surgical bioprostheses.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Valve-in-valve implantation within an existing bioprosthesis may actually result in a smaller effective orifice area due to the nested valve design, potentially worsening the hemodynamic profile.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Both bioprosthetic surgical and transcatheter valves in the tricuspid position typically require some period of anticoagulation. Neither eliminates the need entirely.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A transcatheter tricuspid valve-in-valve procedure does not simultaneously address left-sided valve disease. Separate interventions would be needed.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman presents with progressive lower extremity edema and abdominal distension. She has a history of breast cancer treated with radiation therapy 20 years ago. On examination, JVP is elevated at 16 cmH₂O with a prominent y descent. There is a pericardial knock. Echocardiography shows thickened pericardium, normal LV function, and moderate tricuspid regurgitation. Which of the following is the primary mechanism of the tricuspid regurgitation in this patient?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Pulmonary hypertension from radiation-induced lung fibrosis

choices.label: C
choices.text: RV diastolic dysfunction from constrictive pericarditis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Direct radiation injury to the tricuspid annulus

choices.label: E
choices.text: RV volume overload from an ASD created by radiation

explanation.summary: This patient has radiation-induced constrictive pericarditis. The constricted pericardium impairs RV filling and alters RV geometry, causing functional TR. The pericardial knock and prominent y descent are classic findings of constriction.
explanation.whyCorrect: Constrictive pericarditis from radiation (a late complication appearing years to decades later) restricts RV filling. The thickened pericardium prevents normal RV expansion, altering ventricular geometry and papillary muscle alignment, which impairs tricuspid leaflet coaptation and causes functional TR. The primary treatment is pericardiectomy, which can improve the TR.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While radiation can directly damage valve tissue (fibrosis, calcification), the echocardiographic finding of thickened pericardium with a pericardial knock points to constrictive pericarditis as the primary pathology, with TR as a secondary consequence.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Radiation-induced lung fibrosis can cause pulmonary hypertension, but the clinical picture with pericardial knock and prominent y descent specifically indicates constrictive pericarditis as the dominant process.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Direct radiation injury to the tricuspid annulus is not a recognized primary mechanism. The annular and leaflet changes are secondary to the constrictive physiology.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Radiation does not create atrial septal defects. ASD is a congenital anomaly, not a complication of radiation therapy.

questions.stem: A 38-year-old woman with carcinoid heart disease has severe tricuspid regurgitation and tricuspid stenosis. Her 24-hour urinary 5-HIAA level is markedly elevated. She is scheduled for tricuspid valve replacement. Prior to surgery, which of the following preoperative measures is most important to prevent carcinoid crisis during the procedure?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Preoperative plasma exchange to remove circulating serotonin

explanation.summary: Perioperative octreotide infusion is essential to prevent carcinoid crisis (life-threatening vasodilation, bronchospasm, and hemodynamic collapse) during surgery. Octreotide, a somatostatin analog, inhibits release of vasoactive mediators from the carcinoid tumor.
explanation.whyCorrect: Surgical manipulation, anesthesia, and stress can trigger massive release of serotonin, histamine, and other vasoactive substances from carcinoid tumors, causing carcinoid crisis. IV octreotide (bolus followed by continuous infusion) is started preoperatively and continued throughout surgery. It binds somatostatin receptors on tumor cells, inhibiting hormone secretion.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Corticosteroids are not the standard prophylaxis for carcinoid crisis. They are used for adrenal insufficiency or some allergic reactions but do not prevent mediator release from carcinoid tumors.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Antihistamines may be given as adjunctive therapy but are not the primary prevention strategy. Octreotide addresses the root cause (mediator release) rather than blocking a single mediator.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Plasma exchange is not a standard or practical approach to removing circulating serotonin before surgery. The continuous production by the tumor would rapidly replenish levels.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Famotidine (H2 blocker) may block some histamine-mediated effects but does not prevent the release of serotonin and other mediators that cause carcinoid crisis.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with a permanent transvenous pacemaker implanted 8 years ago presents with progressive lower extremity edema and exertional dyspnea. Echocardiography shows severe tricuspid regurgitation with the pacemaker lead traversing the tricuspid valve. The leaflets appear tethered and restricted by the lead. RV function is moderately reduced. Which of the following is the most likely mechanism of TR in this patient?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Lead-induced leaflet impingement and failure of coaptation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Infective endocarditis of the pacemaker lead

choices.label: C
choices.text: RV dilation from chronic pacing-induced cardiomyopathy

choices.label: D
choices.text: Lead perforation through the RV free wall

choices.label: E
choices.text: Allergic reaction to the lead material causing leaflet inflammation

explanation.summary: Transvenous pacemaker and ICD leads crossing the tricuspid valve are a recognized cause of TR. The lead can directly impinge on leaflets, prevent coaptation, cause fibrosis and adherence of leaflets to the lead, or restrict leaflet mobility.
explanation.whyCorrect: Chronic lead-related TR occurs through several mechanisms: direct mechanical interference with leaflet coaptation (lead crossing between leaflets), lead-induced fibrosis and scarring causing leaflet retraction, and lead adherence to leaflets restricting their motion. The echocardiographic description of tethered, restricted leaflets in the path of the lead supports this mechanism. This is increasingly recognized as a significant clinical problem.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Lead endocarditis would present with fever, positive blood cultures, and vegetations on the lead. The presentation here is chronic and progressive without infectious signs.
whyWrongByChoice.C: RV pacing-induced cardiomyopathy can cause LV dysfunction and secondary MR, but the described mechanism here is direct lead interference with the tricuspid valve, not global cardiomyopathy.
whyWrongByChoice.D: RV perforation typically presents acutely or subacutely with pericardial effusion, tamponade, or lead dysfunction, not chronic progressive TR with leaflet tethering.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Allergic reactions to pacemaker leads are extremely rare and would present with systemic symptoms, not isolated chronic leaflet restriction.

questions.stem: A 10-year-old boy from sub-Saharan Africa presents with fever, migratory polyarthritis of the large joints, and a new heart murmur 3 weeks after a sore throat. His anti-streptolysin O (ASO) titer is elevated. ECG shows a prolonged PR interval of 240 ms. Which of the following pathologic findings would most likely be seen in the myocardium at this stage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Caseating granulomas with Langhans giant cells

choices.label: B
choices.text: Contraction band necrosis with coagulative necrosis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Fibrinoid necrosis of small arterioles with onion-skinning

explanation.summary: This patient meets Jones criteria for acute rheumatic fever (migratory polyarthritis, carditis with new murmur and PR prolongation, elevated ASO titer). The pathognomonic histologic finding of rheumatic carditis is the Aschoff body containing Anitschkow cells.
explanation.whyCorrect: Aschoff bodies are granulomatous lesions found in the myocardium during acute rheumatic carditis. They contain central fibrinoid necrosis surrounded by activated macrophages (Aschoff giant cells) and modified histiocytes called Anitschkow cells, which have distinctive 'caterpillar' or 'owl-eye' nuclei with a wavy chromatin pattern. These are pathognomonic for rheumatic heart disease.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Caseating granulomas with Langhans giant cells are characteristic of tuberculosis, not rheumatic fever.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Contraction band necrosis occurs in reperfusion injury after myocardial infarction or catecholamine-induced myocardial injury, not in rheumatic carditis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Fibrinoid necrosis with onion-skinning of arterioles is the hallmark of malignant hypertension, not rheumatic carditis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Non-caseating granulomas with asteroid bodies are seen in sarcoidosis (cardiac sarcoidosis), not rheumatic fever.

questions.stem: A 12-year-old girl presents with involuntary, purposeless, jerky movements of her hands and face that worsen with stress and disappear during sleep. She had a sore throat 2 months ago that was untreated. Her ASO titer is elevated, and ESR is mildly elevated. Cardiac examination is normal. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: Sydenham chorea (St. Vitus dance) is a major Jones criterion for ARF. It presents as involuntary, non-rhythmic movements that worsen with stress and resolve during sleep, typically appearing 1-6 months after GAS pharyngitis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Sydenham chorea results from anti-neuronal antibodies (molecular mimicry between GAS antigens and basal ganglia neurons) that cross-react with the caudate nucleus and subthalamic nuclei. It is the only major Jones criterion that can stand alone for the diagnosis of ARF, as it may appear months after the initial infection when other inflammatory markers have normalized. It is self-limiting but may take months to resolve.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Tourette syndrome involves motor and vocal tics (not chorea) that are stereotyped and repetitive, unlike the random, flowing movements of chorea. There is no association with streptococcal infection.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Huntington disease is an autosomal dominant neurodegenerative disorder with CAG trinucleotide repeat expansion in the huntingtin gene. It presents in adults (30-50 years), not children, and has a progressive course.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Wilson disease can cause movement disorders including chorea, but it also presents with hepatic dysfunction, Kayser-Fleischer rings, and low ceruloplasmin. The temporal association with pharyngitis and elevated ASO makes Sydenham chorea more likely.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Essential tremor is a rhythmic, bilateral postural/action tremor, distinct from the irregular, purposeless movements of chorea.

questions.stem: A 9-year-old boy is being evaluated for suspected acute rheumatic fever after a confirmed group A streptococcal pharyngitis. He has a temperature of 38.8°C, migratory polyarthritis of the knees and ankles, and a new apical pansystolic murmur. His ESR is 85 mm/hr and CRP is 12 mg/dL. According to the revised Jones criteria (2015), how many major criteria does this patient fulfill?

explanation.summary: This patient has two major Jones criteria: (1) carditis (new apical pansystolic murmur indicating MR) and (2) migratory polyarthritis. Fever and elevated ESR/CRP are minor criteria. He meets diagnostic criteria for ARF (2 major + evidence of prior GAS infection).
explanation.whyCorrect: The 2015 revised Jones criteria have five major criteria: carditis (clinical or subclinical), polyarthritis (migratory in low-risk populations), chorea, erythema marginatum, and subcutaneous nodules. This patient has carditis (new MR murmur) and migratory polyarthritis = 2 major criteria. Fever and elevated acute phase reactants are minor criteria. Two major criteria + evidence of preceding GAS infection (confirmed pharyngitis) fulfills the diagnostic requirement.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The patient has two identifiable major criteria (carditis and polyarthritis), not just one.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Four major criteria would require additional findings like chorea, erythema marginatum, or subcutaneous nodules, none of which are described.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Only two major criteria are present. Fever is a minor criterion, not a major one. No chorea, erythema marginatum, or subcutaneous nodules are described.
whyWrongByChoice.E: This is incorrect — the new murmur represents carditis and the migratory joint involvement represents polyarthritis, both of which are major criteria.

questions.stem: A 14-year-old girl is diagnosed with acute rheumatic fever with carditis and polyarthritis. After treatment of the acute episode, she recovers fully with no residual valvular disease on echocardiography. Which of the following is the most appropriate secondary prophylaxis regimen?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Intramuscular benzathine penicillin G every 4 weeks for 5 years or until age 21

choices.label: B
choices.text: Oral amoxicillin 500 mg twice daily for 1 year

choices.label: C
choices.text: No prophylaxis needed since there is no residual valve disease

choices.label: D
choices.text: Oral azithromycin 250 mg daily for 10 years

choices.label: E
choices.text: Intramuscular benzathine penicillin G every 4 weeks for life

explanation.summary: For ARF with carditis but no residual heart disease (no persistent valvular dysfunction), secondary prophylaxis with IM benzathine penicillin G every 4 weeks (or every 3 weeks in high-risk areas) should continue for 10 years or until age 21, whichever is longer.
explanation.whyCorrect: Secondary prophylaxis prevents recurrent GAS pharyngitis and subsequent rheumatic fever recurrences, which cause cumulative cardiac damage. For carditis without residual heart disease, the AHA recommends prophylaxis for 10 years or until age 21 (whichever is longer). The 5-year minimum applies to ARF without carditis. IM benzathine penicillin G is preferred over oral agents due to superior adherence and efficacy.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Oral amoxicillin is used for treatment of acute GAS pharyngitis, not for long-term secondary prophylaxis. If oral prophylaxis is used, it is penicillin V 250 mg BID, not amoxicillin.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Even without residual heart disease, secondary prophylaxis is required because recurrent ARF episodes carry the risk of causing new or worsening cardiac damage.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Azithromycin is a second-line option for patients with penicillin allergy for acute treatment, but it is not recommended for long-term secondary prophylaxis due to resistance concerns.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Lifelong prophylaxis is recommended for patients with persistent valvular disease (especially severe valve disease or prior valve surgery), not for those with carditis that resolves without residual heart disease.

questions.stem: A 15-year-old boy with acute rheumatic fever develops carditis. Echocardiography shows moderate mitral regurgitation with thickened mitral valve leaflets. Which of the following is the most common valvular lesion in the acute phase of rheumatic carditis?

explanation.summary: Mitral regurgitation is the most common valvular manifestation of acute rheumatic carditis. Valvulitis causes leaflet edema and inflammation, preventing proper coaptation and causing regurgitation. Stenosis develops later from chronic fibrosis and scarring.
explanation.whyCorrect: During acute rheumatic carditis, the inflammatory process causes leaflet edema, verrucous vegetations along the lines of closure, and chordal inflammation, all leading to mitral valve incompetence. The mitral valve is the most commonly affected valve (present in ~75% of rheumatic carditis cases). Stenosis is a chronic sequela that develops over years to decades from progressive fibrosis, calcification, and commissural fusion.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Mitral stenosis is the classic chronic (not acute) manifestation of rheumatic heart disease, developing years after recurrent episodes of rheumatic fever due to progressive scarring and commissural fusion.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Aortic stenosis from rheumatic disease develops chronically. In the acute phase, the aortic valve may show regurgitation but is less commonly affected than the mitral valve.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Tricuspid stenosis is rare and occurs only in the setting of severe rheumatic involvement of multiple valves. It is almost never an isolated or primary acute finding.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Aortic regurgitation can occur in acute rheumatic carditis but is less common than mitral regurgitation. The aortic valve is the second most commonly affected valve.

questions.stem: A 7-year-old girl presents with pink, ring-shaped skin lesions on her trunk that are non-pruritic and blanch with pressure. The rings have clear centers and spread centrifugally. She had a sore throat 2 weeks ago. Which of the following best describes this skin finding?

explanation.summary: Erythema marginatum is a major Jones criterion for ARF. It presents as evanescent, non-pruritic, pink-to-red annular or serpiginous lesions on the trunk and proximal extremities (sparing the face) that blanch with pressure and may be accentuated by warmth.
explanation.whyCorrect: Erythema marginatum is characterized by erythematous rings with pale centers that migrate outward, creating a serpiginous pattern. The lesions are transient (may appear and disappear within hours), non-pruritic, and found primarily on the trunk. It is one of the five major Jones criteria and is highly specific for ARF, though it is uncommon (seen in <5% of cases).

whyWrongByChoice.A: Erythema nodosum presents as tender, red-purple nodules on the anterior shins (not ring-shaped trunk lesions). It is associated with sarcoidosis, IBD, infections, and medications.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Erythema multiforme presents with target (iris) lesions — concentric rings of color change — often on the palms and extremities. It is associated with HSV and medications, not streptococcal infection.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Erythema chronicum migrans is the expanding bull's-eye rash of Lyme disease (Borrelia burgdorferi), appearing at the tick bite site. It is a single expanding lesion, not multiple migratory rings.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Annular urticaria presents with pruritic, edematous wheals that can be ring-shaped. Erythema marginatum is distinctly non-pruritic, differentiating it from urticaria.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old man from India with known rheumatic heart disease and severe mitral stenosis develops a recurrence of ARF with high fever and worsening heart failure. Despite appropriate antibiotic therapy and medical management, his condition deteriorates over the next 48 hours with progressive pulmonary edema and hemodynamic instability. Which of the following is the most appropriate management?

explanation.summary: When recurrent ARF causes hemodynamic decompensation from severe valvular disease refractory to medical therapy, emergent valve surgery is indicated. Active rheumatic carditis is not an absolute contraindication to surgery when the patient's life is threatened by valve dysfunction.
explanation.whyCorrect: Although surgery during active rheumatic carditis carries higher risk of valve failure and recurrence, it is lifesaving when medical management fails and the patient is in cardiogenic shock. The valve disease (severe MS with hemodynamic instability) is the immediate life-threatening problem requiring mechanical intervention.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Corticosteroids may reduce inflammation in acute rheumatic carditis but have not been shown to prevent valvular damage or improve outcomes when hemodynamic instability is driven by severe mechanical valve disease.
whyWrongByChoice.B: IVIG has not been established as effective therapy for acute rheumatic fever or its cardiac complications. There is no evidence supporting its use in this setting.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Plasma exchange is not a standard therapy for ARF. While it is used in some autoimmune conditions, removing circulating antibodies does not address the established valvular damage causing hemodynamic collapse.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Cyclophosphamide is an immunosuppressive agent used in severe autoimmune conditions (e.g., lupus nephritis, vasculitis) but has no role in the management of acute rheumatic fever.

questions.stem: A pathologist examines a mitral valve specimen from a patient with chronic rheumatic heart disease. The valve shows leaflet thickening, commissural fusion, chordal shortening, and small (1-2 mm) verrucous vegetations along the line of closure. Which of the following best describes these vegetations?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Large, friable vegetations composed of bacteria and fibrin

choices.label: B
choices.text: Small, sterile, fibrin-platelet deposits along the closure line

choices.label: C
choices.text: Bulky vegetations with ring abscess and valve perforation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Flat, broad-based vegetations on the atrial surface (Libman-Sacks)

explanation.summary: The verrucae of rheumatic valve disease are small (1-2 mm), firm, sterile vegetations composed of fibrin and platelets deposited along the lines of valve closure. They form due to endothelial damage from the inflammatory process.
explanation.whyCorrect: Rheumatic verrucous vegetations are small, warty deposits that form along the valve closure line where the leaflets undergo repeated trauma. They are sterile (no organisms) and consist of fibrin and platelets. Unlike infective endocarditis vegetations, they are small, firmly attached, and rarely embolize. They reflect endocardial inflammation and damage from the autoimmune process.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Large, friable vegetations containing bacteria and fibrin are characteristic of infective endocarditis, not rheumatic disease. IE vegetations are much larger and prone to embolization.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Bulky vegetations with ring abscess and valve perforation are seen in aggressive acute IE (especially Staphylococcus aureus), not in rheumatic heart disease.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Libman-Sacks vegetations (seen in SLE) are flat, broad-based, and occur on both surfaces of the valve (especially the underside/ventricular surface). They differ from the small verrucae of RHD that are limited to the closure line.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Large pedunculated myxomatous masses describe cardiac myxomas or papillary fibroelastomas, not rheumatic valve disease.

questions.stem: A 8-year-old boy presents with sore throat, fever of 38.5°C, and tonsillar exudates. Rapid streptococcal antigen test is positive. He has no drug allergies. Which of the following is the most appropriate treatment to prevent acute rheumatic fever?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Symptomatic treatment only, as the infection is self-limited

explanation.summary: Primary prevention of ARF requires adequate treatment of GAS pharyngitis. Oral amoxicillin (or penicillin V) for 10 days is the first-line treatment. Adequate duration is critical to eradicate GAS and prevent the immune-mediated sequelae.
explanation.whyCorrect: A full 10-day course of oral amoxicillin (50 mg/kg/day, max 1000 mg/day) or penicillin V is the recommended first-line treatment for GAS pharyngitis to prevent ARF. The 10-day duration is necessary to completely eradicate GAS from the pharynx. Alternatively, a single IM injection of benzathine penicillin G can be used.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Azithromycin (5-day course) is a second-line option reserved for patients with penicillin allergy. Increasing GAS resistance to macrolides makes this a less reliable choice. A 3-day course would be inadequate.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A 5-day course of amoxicillin is insufficient to eradicate GAS pharyngitis. Studies consistently show that 10 days are needed to prevent ARF, even though symptoms may resolve earlier.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While GAS pharyngitis is self-limited symptomatically, untreated infection carries a 3% risk of developing ARF. Antibiotic treatment is essential for primary prevention.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Topical throat sprays provide symptomatic relief but do not eradicate the streptococcal organism and cannot prevent ARF.

questions.stem: A 11-year-old boy with acute rheumatic fever has severe carditis with heart failure. He is treated with bed rest, penicillin, and diuretics. His physician is considering anti-inflammatory therapy. Which of the following anti-inflammatory agents is most appropriate for rheumatic carditis with heart failure?

explanation.summary: For severe rheumatic carditis with heart failure, corticosteroids (prednisone 1-2 mg/kg/day) are recommended over aspirin alone. Corticosteroids provide more potent anti-inflammatory effects and are preferred when there is significant cardiac inflammation.
explanation.whyCorrect: While high-dose aspirin is the first-line anti-inflammatory for arthritis in ARF, severe carditis with heart failure warrants corticosteroids for more potent and rapid anti-inflammatory effects. Prednisone is started at 1-2 mg/kg/day (max 80 mg) and tapered over 2-3 weeks, often overlapping with aspirin to prevent rebound inflammation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: High-dose aspirin is appropriate for rheumatic arthritis and mild carditis, but severe carditis with heart failure requires the more potent anti-inflammatory effect of corticosteroids.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Ibuprofen may be used for arthritis in ARF if aspirin is not tolerated, but it lacks the evidence base and potency of corticosteroids for severe carditis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Colchicine is used for acute pericarditis and gout, not for rheumatic carditis. It has no established role in the management of ARF.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Indomethacin is not a standard therapy for rheumatic carditis. It is used for other inflammatory conditions (e.g., pericarditis, gout) but not recommended for ARF management.

questions.stem: A 13-year-old girl presents with painless, firm, mobile subcutaneous nodules over the extensor surfaces of her elbows, knees, and occiput. She has a history of recurrent rheumatic fever. Which of the following statements about subcutaneous nodules in rheumatic fever is correct?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They are the most common major manifestation of ARF

choices.label: B
choices.text: They typically precede all other manifestations by several weeks

choices.label: C
choices.text: They are strongly associated with severe carditis

choices.label: D
choices.text: They are pathognomonic for ARF and not seen in other conditions

choices.label: E
choices.text: They are typically painful and erythematous

explanation.summary: Subcutaneous nodules in ARF are rare (<5% of cases) but are strongly associated with severe carditis. Their presence should prompt careful cardiac evaluation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Subcutaneous nodules are small (0.5-2 cm), painless, firm, and found over bony prominences and extensor tendons. They are among the least common major criteria but carry significant clinical importance because they almost always occur in patients with severe carditis. Histologically, they resemble Aschoff bodies with central fibrinoid necrosis surrounded by inflammatory cells.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Subcutaneous nodules are one of the least common manifestations (<5%). The most common major criterion is polyarthritis (~75% of cases), followed by carditis (~50-60%).
whyWrongByChoice.B: Subcutaneous nodules typically appear during the active phase of ARF or even later, not before other manifestations. They usually coincide with or follow carditis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Subcutaneous nodules are not pathognomonic for ARF. Similar nodules can be found in rheumatoid arthritis (rheumatoid nodules), SLE, and granuloma annulare. However, in the context of Jones criteria, they support the diagnosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Subcutaneous nodules of ARF are characteristically painless and non-tender, not painful or erythematous. Painful nodules suggest other diagnoses such as erythema nodosum.

questions.stem: A 16-year-old boy with a history of ARF 4 years ago (with carditis and residual moderate MR) has been receiving benzathine penicillin G injections every 4 weeks. He now requires a dental extraction. Which of the following is the most appropriate approach regarding endocarditis prophylaxis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No additional prophylaxis needed since he is already on penicillin

choices.label: B
choices.text: Give amoxicillin 2g one hour before the procedure

choices.label: C
choices.text: Give clindamycin 600 mg one hour before the procedure

choices.label: D
choices.text: Give amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg twice daily for 7 days

choices.label: E
choices.text: Give vancomycin 1g intravenously one hour before the procedure

explanation.summary: This patient is already on penicillin for rheumatic fever prophylaxis. If IE prophylaxis is indicated, a different antibiotic class should be used (e.g., clindamycin) because oral streptococci may have developed penicillin resistance from chronic penicillin exposure.
explanation.whyCorrect: Patients on chronic penicillin for ARF prophylaxis may harbor penicillin-resistant viridans group streptococci in the oral flora. If endocarditis prophylaxis is warranted for a dental procedure, clindamycin (or azithromycin/clarithromycin) should be used instead of amoxicillin to provide coverage against potentially resistant organisms. Note: current AHA guidelines do not specifically recommend IE prophylaxis for native valve rheumatic disease without prosthetic material, but the principle of using an alternative class is important.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The monthly benzathine penicillin provides continuous low-level penicillin but does not achieve the high transient bactericidal levels needed for IE prophylaxis. Additionally, oral flora may be resistant to penicillin.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Amoxicillin is the standard first-line IE prophylaxis for patients not on chronic penicillin. In patients already receiving penicillin prophylaxis, oral streptococci may be resistant, making amoxicillin unreliable.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A 7-day course of amoxicillin-clavulanate is not the standard regimen for IE prophylaxis. Single-dose pre-procedural prophylaxis is the accepted approach, and the penicillin resistance concern still applies.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Intravenous vancomycin is unnecessarily aggressive for routine dental prophylaxis. It is reserved for patients who cannot take oral medications and have penicillin allergy. Oral clindamycin is simpler and appropriate.

questions.stem: A 10-year-old boy presents with acute rheumatic fever. His joints show migratory polyarthritis affecting the knees, ankles, and wrists. The arthritis in each joint lasts 2-3 days before migrating. Which of the following statements about rheumatic arthritis is correct?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Joint involvement causes permanent erosive damage

choices.label: B
choices.text: Small joints of the hands and feet are most commonly affected

choices.label: C
choices.text: NSAIDs should be avoided due to risk of Reye syndrome

choices.label: D
choices.text: The arthritis and carditis have equal risk of causing permanent damage

choices.label: E
choices.text: The arthritis responds dramatically to aspirin within 24-48 hours

explanation.summary: Rheumatic arthritis characteristically responds rapidly and dramatically to aspirin (or NSAIDs), with significant improvement within 24-48 hours. This rapid response is so typical that failure to respond should prompt reconsideration of the diagnosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: The dramatic response of rheumatic arthritis to salicylates is a hallmark feature. Aspirin at anti-inflammatory doses (80-100 mg/kg/day) produces rapid resolution of joint pain and swelling. If the arthritis does not respond within 48 hours, alternative diagnoses (septic arthritis, reactive arthritis, juvenile idiopathic arthritis) should be considered.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Rheumatic arthritis is non-erosive and completely reversible, leaving no permanent joint damage. This distinguishes it from rheumatoid arthritis, which causes joint erosions and deformity.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Rheumatic arthritis preferentially affects large joints (knees, ankles, wrists, elbows), not the small joints of the hands and feet. Small joint involvement is characteristic of rheumatoid arthritis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: While Reye syndrome is a concern with aspirin use in children with viral infections (especially influenza, varicella), aspirin is specifically recommended for rheumatic fever arthritis. The risk-benefit ratio favors aspirin in this context.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Rheumatic arthritis leaves no permanent damage ('licks the joints'), while carditis 'bites the heart' — causing potentially permanent valvular damage. They carry very different risks for long-term sequelae.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman from Southeast Asia with chronic rheumatic heart disease presents with dyspnea. Echocardiography reveals severe mitral stenosis with a valve area of 0.8 cm² and moderate aortic regurgitation with leaflet retraction. The mechanism of rheumatic valvular disease involves molecular mimicry between group A streptococcal antigens and host tissues. Which of the following streptococcal components is most directly involved in this molecular mimicry?

explanation.summary: The M protein of group A Streptococcus shares epitopes with cardiac myosin, valve tissue glycoproteins, and brain tissue (basal ganglia). Antibodies generated against M protein cross-react with these host tissues, driving the autoimmune damage in ARF.
explanation.whyCorrect: M protein is the principal virulence factor of GAS and the key mediator of molecular mimicry in rheumatic fever. Specific M protein serotypes (e.g., M1, M3, M5, M6, M18) are considered 'rheumatogenic.' Anti-M protein antibodies cross-react with cardiac myosin, laminin, and valve endothelial surface proteins, triggering complement activation and T-cell mediated damage to the valve endothelium, myocardium, and other tissues.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Streptolysin O is a pore-forming cytolysin that triggers the ASO antibody response used diagnostically. While ASO titers confirm recent GAS infection, streptolysin O itself is not the primary mediator of molecular mimicry with cardiac tissue.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Hyaluronidase is a spreading factor that degrades hyaluronic acid in connective tissue, facilitating bacterial invasion. It is not a major target of the cross-reactive autoimmune response.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Streptokinase activates plasminogen to promote fibrinolysis and bacterial spread but is not the primary component involved in molecular mimicry with cardiac tissue.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The GAS carbohydrate antigen (N-acetylglucosamine) may share some structural similarity with glycosaminoglycans on heart valves, but the M protein is the dominant antigen mediating molecular mimicry in rheumatic heart disease.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old man with a history of intravenous heroin use presents with a 2-week history of fever, malaise, and night sweats. His temperature is 39.0°C, heart rate 110 bpm. Examination reveals splinter hemorrhages, Janeway lesions on the palms, and a new holosystolic murmur at the apex. Blood cultures grow gram-positive cocci in clusters. Which of the following organisms is most likely responsible?

explanation.summary: S. aureus is the most common cause of IE in IV drug users and the most common overall cause of IE in developed countries. Gram-positive cocci in clusters are characteristic of staphylococci. The acute presentation with embolic phenomena (Janeway lesions) is typical of S. aureus IE.
explanation.whyCorrect: S. aureus causes acute, aggressive endocarditis with large, friable vegetations prone to embolization. In IVDU, it classically affects the tricuspid valve, but can also affect left-sided valves as in this case (apical murmur suggesting mitral involvement). Janeway lesions (painless erythematous lesions on palms/soles from septic emboli) are characteristic of S. aureus IE.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Viridans group streptococci cause subacute IE (insidious onset over weeks to months) in patients with pre-existing valve disease. They are gram-positive cocci in chains, not clusters.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Enterococcus faecalis is associated with IE in elderly men after GU procedures. Enterococci are gram-positive cocci in chains or pairs, not clusters.
whyWrongByChoice.D: S. epidermidis is the most common cause of prosthetic valve endocarditis (especially early PVE within 60 days), not native valve endocarditis in IVDU.
whyWrongByChoice.E: S. bovis (now S. gallolyticus) causes subacute IE and is strongly associated with colorectal malignancy. It presents as gram-positive cocci in chains.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with a mechanical aortic valve prosthesis implanted 3 weeks ago presents with fever, chills, and a new regurgitant murmur. Blood cultures grow coagulase-negative staphylococci. TEE shows a small vegetation on the prosthetic valve with a paravalvular abscess. Which of the following is the most appropriate management?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Vancomycin plus gentamicin plus rifampin with planned 6-week IV course

choices.label: C
choices.text: Vancomycin plus gentamicin plus rifampin with early surgical intervention

explanation.summary: Early prosthetic valve endocarditis (<60 days post-surgery) with paravalvular abscess requires both aggressive antimicrobial therapy (vancomycin + gentamicin + rifampin for CoNS) and early surgical intervention. Paravalvular abscess is a class I indication for surgery.
explanation.whyCorrect: Early PVE is most commonly caused by coagulase-negative staphylococci (usually methicillin-resistant). Treatment requires vancomycin (cell wall activity), rifampin (biofilm penetration, critical for prosthetic infections), and gentamicin (synergistic killing). The paravalvular abscess is a class I surgical indication because antibiotics alone cannot eradicate infection in avascular abscess cavities, and extension risks atrioventricular block and dehiscence.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The antibiotic combination is correct, but medical therapy alone is insufficient when a paravalvular abscess is present. Surgery is indicated.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Nafcillin is a beta-lactam effective against MSSA but most CoNS in early PVE are methicillin-resistant (mecA positive). Additionally, rifampin is essential for prosthetic valve infections to penetrate biofilm.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Daptomycin monotherapy is not recommended for PVE. It lacks the biofilm-penetrating activity of rifampin and does not address the need for surgical intervention with abscess.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Ceftriaxone plus gentamicin is used for streptococcal IE, not staphylococcal PVE. This regimen lacks activity against methicillin-resistant CoNS and biofilm.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with a history of aortic valve endocarditis treated medically 6 weeks ago presents with recurrent fevers. ECG now shows a new first-degree AV block (PR interval 280 ms) that was not present on prior ECGs. TEE shows a perivalvular abscess extending toward the membranous interventricular septum. Which of the following best explains the new conduction abnormality?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Drug-induced AV block from beta-blocker therapy

choices.label: B
choices.text: Extension of the abscess into the conduction system near the AV node

choices.label: C
choices.text: Vegetation embolization to a coronary artery

choices.label: E
choices.text: Electrolyte disturbance from prolonged IV antibiotic therapy

explanation.summary: A new conduction abnormality (first-degree, second-degree, or third-degree AV block) in aortic valve IE is a red flag for perivalvular abscess extending into the interventricular septum, where the AV node and bundle of His are located.
explanation.whyCorrect: The AV node lies in the triangle of Koch at the base of the interatrial septum, and the bundle of His penetrates the central fibrous body near the membranous septum. Aortic valve abscesses, particularly of the noncoronary cusp, can extend into this area, damaging the conduction system. A new AV block in aortic valve IE has high specificity for perivalvular abscess and is a class I indication for surgical intervention.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Beta-blockers can cause AV block, but the clinical context of recurrent fever with known aortic IE and a TEE-confirmed abscess makes perivalvular extension the most likely cause.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Coronary embolization from vegetations can cause MI but would present with ST changes and chest pain rather than isolated conduction delay.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Drug-induced myocarditis (e.g., from beta-lactam antibiotics) is rare and would present with diffuse ST changes, eosinophilia, and ventricular dysfunction, not isolated AV block with a perivalvular abscess.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Electrolyte disturbances (hyperkalemia, hypocalcemia) can affect conduction, but the TEE finding of a perivalvular abscess provides a clear anatomic explanation.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old woman with no prior cardiac history presents with 6 weeks of low-grade fevers, weight loss, and fatigue. Blood cultures grow Streptococcus gallolyticus (formerly S. bovis biotype I). TEE confirms a vegetation on the aortic valve. In addition to treating the endocarditis, which of the following evaluations is most important?

choices.label: A
choices.text: CT of the chest for occult lung malignancy

choices.label: B
choices.text: Colonoscopy to evaluate for colorectal neoplasia

choices.label: C
choices.text: Bone marrow biopsy for occult hematologic malignancy

choices.label: E
choices.text: Echocardiographic evaluation of all cardiac valves

explanation.summary: S. gallolyticus (S. bovis biotype I) bacteremia and endocarditis have a strong association with colorectal malignancy (adenomas and carcinomas). Colonoscopy is mandatory in all patients with S. gallolyticus bacteremia.
explanation.whyCorrect: The association between S. gallolyticus and colorectal neoplasia is well-established, with up to 25-80% of patients having colonic polyps or carcinoma. The organism colonizes disrupted colonic epithelium overlying neoplastic tissue and translocates into the bloodstream.
