Colonoscopy should be performed in all patients with S. gallolyticus bacteremia, even without GI symptoms.

whyWrongByChoice.A: S. gallolyticus is specifically associated with colorectal (not pulmonary) malignancy. CT chest is not a priority evaluation triggered by this organism.
whyWrongByChoice.C: There is no specific association between S. gallolyticus and hematologic malignancies. Bone marrow biopsy is not indicated.
whyWrongByChoice.D: HIV and hepatitis testing may be appropriate in certain contexts but are not specifically prompted by S. gallolyticus bacteremia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Echocardiographic evaluation is part of standard IE workup and has already been performed (TEE showing aortic valve vegetation). While important, it does not address the critical malignancy screening needed.

questions.stem: A 42-year-old woman presents with fever and a new mitral regurgitation murmur. Three sets of blood cultures drawn over 24 hours are negative after 5 days of incubation. She recently returned from rural France where she had contact with farm animals including cats. TEE shows a 12 mm vegetation on the mitral valve. Serologic testing reveals high antibody titers against a small intracellular gram-negative organism. Which of the following is the most likely causative organism?

explanation.summary: Culture-negative endocarditis with farm animal/cat exposure and high antibody titers against an intracellular gram-negative organism suggests Bartonella henselae. Bartonella is one of the most common causes of culture-negative IE and is transmitted by cat scratches/fleas.
explanation.whyCorrect: Bartonella species (B. henselae from cats, B. quintana in homeless patients) are fastidious, intracellular gram-negative bacteria that are a leading cause of culture-negative IE. They do not grow on standard blood culture media. Diagnosis relies on serology (IFA titers), PCR of blood or valve tissue, or Warthin-Starry silver stain of valve tissue. Cat exposure is the key epidemiologic clue for B. henselae.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Brucella can cause culture-negative IE (requires prolonged incubation) and is associated with farm animal exposure, but it typically causes undulant fever and is not described as a small intracellular organism diagnosed primarily by serology in the same clinical context as Bartonella.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Coxiella burnetii (Q fever) can also cause culture-negative IE and is associated with farm animals (cattle, sheep, goats), but is transmitted by inhalation of contaminated aerosols, not by cat contact. Q fever endocarditis typically affects abnormal valves and has a chronic course.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Legionella pneumophila primarily causes pneumonia, not endocarditis. It grows on BCYE agar, not by serology for intracellular organisms.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Tropheryma whipplei causes Whipple disease with GI symptoms (diarrhea, malabsorption, arthralgias) and rarely IE. There are no GI symptoms described here.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman with a bicuspid aortic valve is planning a dental extraction. She has no history of endocarditis. According to current AHA guidelines, which of the following patients requires antibiotic prophylaxis before dental procedures?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A patient with a bicuspid aortic valve

choices.label: B
choices.text: A patient with mitral valve prolapse and severe MR

choices.label: C
choices.text: A patient with rheumatic mitral stenosis

choices.label: D
choices.text: A patient with a previous episode of infective endocarditis

explanation.summary: Current AHA guidelines (2007, reaffirmed) restrict IE prophylaxis to the highest-risk patients: prior IE, prosthetic valves (including TAVR), unrepaired cyanotic CHD, repaired CHD with residual defects, and cardiac transplant recipients with valvulopathy.
explanation.whyCorrect: Prior IE is one of the specific high-risk conditions for which prophylaxis is recommended before dental procedures involving manipulation of gingival tissue, the periapical region, or perforation of the oral mucosa. These patients have the highest risk of recurrent IE and the worst outcomes if IE develops.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Bicuspid aortic valve, even with stenosis or regurgitation, is no longer an indication for IE prophylaxis under current guidelines. The 2007 AHA revision eliminated prophylaxis for most native valvular conditions.
whyWrongByChoice.B: MVP with severe MR was previously considered for prophylaxis but is no longer recommended under current AHA guidelines.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Rheumatic valve disease, including mitral stenosis, is not included in the current high-risk categories for IE prophylaxis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy is not a current indication for IE prophylaxis, regardless of the presence of obstruction.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with native aortic valve endocarditis due to Staphylococcus aureus has been on appropriate IV antibiotics for 10 days. On day 11, he develops sudden left-sided weakness. CT angiogram of the head shows a large mycotic aneurysm of the right middle cerebral artery with surrounding hemorrhage. Which of the following is the most appropriate management of the intracranial lesion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Continue antibiotics alone and observe with serial imaging

choices.label: B
choices.text: Immediate cardiac surgery followed by craniotomy

choices.label: E
choices.text: Endovascular coiling or surgical clipping of the mycotic aneurysm

explanation.summary: A ruptured mycotic aneurysm with intracranial hemorrhage requires urgent intervention — endovascular coiling (preferred when anatomically feasible) or surgical clipping. Unruptured mycotic aneurysms may be monitored with antibiotics, but rupture is a neurosurgical emergency.
explanation.whyCorrect: Mycotic aneurysms result from septic embolization to the vasa vasorum of cerebral arteries, causing arterial wall weakening. When ruptured (as evidenced by hemorrhage), urgent intervention is needed to prevent rebleeding and expansion. Endovascular coiling is increasingly preferred due to its minimally invasive nature. Cardiac surgery should be delayed at least 4 weeks after intracranial hemorrhage if possible.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Observation is appropriate for small, unruptured mycotic aneurysms that may resolve with antibiotics. However, a ruptured aneurysm with hemorrhage requires active intervention to prevent fatal rebleeding.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Cardiac surgery should be delayed after intracranial hemorrhage due to the need for heparinization during cardiopulmonary bypass, which could worsen brain hemorrhage. The intracranial lesion should be secured first.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Radiation therapy has no role in treating mycotic aneurysms. This modality is used for malignancies and certain vascular malformations, not infectious vascular complications.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Systemic thrombolysis is absolutely contraindicated in the setting of intracranial hemorrhage, as it would catastrophically worsen the bleeding.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man presents with fever and fatigue for 3 months. He had a colonoscopy with polyp removal 2 months ago. Blood cultures grow Enterococcus faecalis. TEE reveals a 15 mm vegetation on the mitral valve with moderate MR. He has normal renal function. Which of the following antibiotic regimens is most appropriate?

choices.label: E
choices.text: Penicillin G plus streptomycin for 2 weeks

explanation.summary: Enterococcal IE requires prolonged combination therapy (6 weeks). Ampicillin plus ceftriaxone (double beta-lactam synergy) is the preferred regimen for E. faecalis IE, offering synergistic bactericidal activity without nephrotoxic aminoglycosides.
explanation.whyCorrect: Enterococci are intrinsically resistant to cephalosporins alone and tolerant to beta-lactam monotherapy (bacteriostatic, not bactericidal). Synergistic combinations are required. Ampicillin + ceftriaxone achieves synergy by saturating different penicillin-binding proteins (PBPs). This regimen has equal efficacy to ampicillin + gentamicin but avoids nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity, making it especially advantageous in elderly patients.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Vancomycin monotherapy is bacteriostatic against enterococci and is not recommended. Combination therapy is needed for bactericidal activity in IE.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Ceftriaxone alone has no activity against enterococci due to their intrinsic resistance to cephalosporins. It cannot be used as monotherapy.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Daptomycin is not first-line for enterococcal IE. While it may be considered for VRE IE, the standard treatment for ampicillin-susceptible E. faecalis is ampicillin-based combinations.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A 2-week course is inappropriate for enterococcal IE, which requires 6 weeks of therapy. Short-course regimens (2 weeks) are only used for uncomplicated native valve streptococcal IE.

questions.stem: A 44-year-old man with native mitral valve IE caused by S. aureus develops acute left-sided weakness on day 5 of antibiotic therapy. CT head shows an ischemic stroke in the right MCA territory without hemorrhagic transformation. TEE shows a 18 mm mobile vegetation on the anterior mitral leaflet with severe MR. Regarding the timing of cardiac surgery, which of the following is most appropriate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Delay surgery for at least 4 weeks after the stroke

choices.label: B
choices.text: Proceed with early surgery within 1-2 weeks if no hemorrhagic transformation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Surgery is permanently contraindicated after an embolic stroke

choices.label: D
choices.text: Perform surgery only after completing 6 weeks of antibiotics

choices.label: E
choices.text: Immediate surgery within 24 hours regardless of stroke size

explanation.summary: For non-hemorrhagic embolic stroke in the setting of IE with ongoing surgical indications (large mobile vegetation, severe MR), early surgery can proceed within 1-2 weeks if repeat imaging shows no hemorrhagic transformation. Studies show acceptable neurologic outcomes with this approach.
explanation.whyCorrect: Current evidence suggests that non-hemorrhagic ischemic stroke is not a contraindication to early cardiac surgery if the stroke is small-to-moderate and there is no hemorrhagic transformation on repeat imaging. The large vegetation (>15 mm) with severe MR is a class I indication for surgery. Delaying surgery risks recurrent embolization and progressive valve destruction. Most studies show surgery within 1-2 weeks of non-hemorrhagic stroke has acceptable outcomes.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Delaying 4 weeks is recommended after hemorrhagic stroke (to reduce rebleeding risk during cardiopulmonary bypass). For non-hemorrhagic stroke, this prolonged delay risks additional embolic events from the large, mobile vegetation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Stroke is not a permanent contraindication. With appropriate timing and imaging surveillance for hemorrhagic transformation, cardiac surgery can be safely performed.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Waiting 6 weeks to complete antibiotics before surgery ignores the ongoing embolic and hemodynamic risks from the large vegetation and severe MR.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Immediate surgery within 24 hours carries higher neurologic risk from stroke edema and potential hemorrhagic transformation during heparinization. A brief waiting period with repeat imaging is preferred.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman presents with fever, petechiae, splenomegaly, and painful nodules on her fingertips. Blood cultures grow viridans group streptococci. Which of the following clinical findings represents immune complex-mediated vasculitis rather than septic embolization?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Janeway lesions — painless hemorrhagic macules on palms and soles

choices.label: B
choices.text: Osler nodes — painful raised lesions on fingers and toes

choices.label: C
choices.text: Septic pulmonary emboli with cavitating nodules

explanation.summary: Osler nodes are painful, erythematous nodules on the pads of the fingers and toes caused by immune complex deposition and vasculitis. They are distinguished from Janeway lesions, which are painless and result from septic microemboli.
explanation.whyCorrect: Osler nodes represent immune complex-mediated vasculitis in the digital vasculature. Circulating immune complexes deposit in small vessels, activating complement and causing local inflammation and pain. They are more common in subacute IE (e.g., viridans streptococci). Key distinction: Osler = Ouch (painful, immune), Janeway = painless (septic emboli).

whyWrongByChoice.A: Janeway lesions are painless hemorrhagic macules caused by septic microemboli lodging in dermal vessels, leading to small areas of infarction. They are septic, not immune-mediated.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Septic pulmonary emboli represent direct embolization of infected vegetation fragments to the pulmonary vasculature, not immune complex deposition.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Splenic infarction results from direct embolization of vegetation fragments to the splenic artery, causing tissue infarction — a septic embolic event.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Conjunctival hemorrhages (Roth spots when seen in the retina) can be from either emboli or immune complex deposition, but conjunctival petechiae are primarily embolic.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with a bioprosthetic aortic valve implanted 2 years ago presents with persistent fever despite 3 weeks of appropriate IV antibiotics for methicillin-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus endocarditis. Repeat blood cultures remain positive. TEE shows new paravalvular regurgitation. Which of the following Duke criteria categories does persistent bacteremia despite appropriate therapy fall under?

choices.label: E
choices.text: Not part of the Duke criteria

explanation.summary: Persistent bacteremia (positive blood cultures drawn >12 hours apart or all of 3 or more separate cultures positive) is a major microbiological Duke criterion for IE.
explanation.whyCorrect: The modified Duke criteria include two categories of major criteria: microbiological and echocardiographic/imaging. Persistently positive blood cultures (with a typical IE organism from cultures drawn >12 hours apart, or all of ≥3 separate sets positive, or the majority of ≥4 sets positive with at least 1 hour between first and last) qualify as a major microbiological criterion. The continuous bacteremia reflects the intravascular nature of the infection.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Echocardiographic major criteria include vegetation, abscess, prosthetic valve dehiscence, or new valvular regurgitation. The paravalvular regurgitation is a separate echocardiographic major criterion, but the bacteremia itself is classified as microbiological.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Predisposition (predisposing heart condition or IVDU) is a minor Duke criterion, not related to blood culture positivity.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Fever ≥38°C is a minor Duke criterion. While this patient has fever, the persistent bacteremia specifically is classified as a major criterion.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Persistent bacteremia is explicitly included in the Duke criteria as one of the most important diagnostic features.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old man with native mitral valve endocarditis caused by Staphylococcus aureus is being considered for surgery. He has been on antibiotics for 5 days. Which of the following is the strongest indication for early surgical intervention?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Vegetation size of 8 mm without embolization

choices.label: B
choices.text: Fever persisting for 48 hours after starting antibiotics

choices.label: C
choices.text: Heart failure from severe acute mitral regurgitation refractory to medical therapy

choices.label: D
choices.text: Positive blood cultures for 3 days on appropriate antibiotics

explanation.summary: Heart failure from acute severe valve regurgitation or obstruction refractory to medical therapy is the strongest (class I) indication for urgent/emergent surgery in IE. It is the most common indication for surgery and the factor most predictive of poor outcome without intervention.
explanation.whyCorrect: Heart failure is the most common complication of IE and the leading indication for surgery. Acute severe MR or AR causing pulmonary edema or cardiogenic shock refractory to medical management requires emergent surgical intervention regardless of antibiotic duration. The 2015 AHA/ACC guidelines and ESC 2023 guidelines both list this as a class I indication.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Vegetation size of 8 mm without embolization is below the 10 mm threshold generally used for surgery based on vegetation size alone. Vegetation ≥10 mm with additional risk factors (embolic event, specific organisms) is a class I indication.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Persistent fever for 48 hours is too early to conclude treatment failure. Fever can persist for 5-7 days in S. aureus IE before resolving. Persistent fever beyond 7-10 days on appropriate therapy raises concern.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Positive cultures at 3 days may occur with S. aureus IE. Persistently positive cultures beyond 5-7 days on appropriate therapy suggest treatment failure and may indicate surgical need, but 3 days is premature.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Mild splenomegaly is common in IE and reflects the immune response. It is not an indication for surgery unless there is splenic abscess.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old woman with no significant medical history develops Streptococcus gallolyticus endocarditis of the aortic valve. After identification of the organism, colonoscopy reveals a 3 cm tubular adenoma with high-grade dysplasia. After successful treatment of her endocarditis, she asks about the mechanism by which the colonic lesion predisposed her to endocarditis. Which of the following best explains the pathogenesis?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Disruption of the mucosal barrier allowing bacterial translocation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Production of bacteriocins by the adenoma promoting S. gallolyticus growth

choices.label: D
choices.text: Direct hematogenous seeding from tumor neovascularization

choices.label: E
choices.text: Alteration of bile acid metabolism favoring colonic infection

explanation.summary: Colonic neoplasms (adenomas, carcinomas) disrupt the intestinal mucosal barrier, allowing S. gallolyticus (which colonizes the GI tract and has a tropism for dysplastic colonic epithelium) to translocate into the bloodstream and seed the endocardium.
explanation.whyCorrect: S. gallolyticus has specific adhesins (pili, collagen-binding proteins) that facilitate attachment to collagen-rich extracellular matrix exposed by mucosal disruption overlying neoplastic tissue. The tumor disrupts the mucosal barrier, and the organism translocates through the damaged epithelium into the portal and systemic circulation, eventually seeding the cardiac valves. S. gallolyticus also promotes tumor growth through NF-κB-mediated inflammation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Colonic adenomas do not cause systemic immunosuppression. The mechanism is local barrier disruption, not immune compromise.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Bacteriocins are antimicrobial peptides produced by bacteria against competing organisms. There is no mechanism by which adenomas produce bacteriocins. The tropism is due to the organism's adhesins for neoplastic tissue.
whyWrongByChoice.D: While tumor neovascularization provides blood supply to the adenoma, the primary mechanism is mucosal disruption and bacterial translocation, not direct hematogenous spread from tumor vessels.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Bile acid metabolism changes may occur in GI disease but are not the primary mechanism linking S. gallolyticus to colorectal neoplasia and endocarditis.

questions.stem: A 38-year-old man with infective endocarditis of the mitral valve is found to have glomerulonephritis with hematuria, proteinuria, and elevated creatinine. Renal biopsy shows diffuse proliferative glomerulonephritis with 'wire-loop' thickening of the capillary walls and subendothelial immune complex deposits on electron microscopy. Serum complement levels (C3, C4) are low. Which of the following immunologic mechanisms is most directly responsible for his renal disease?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Type I hypersensitivity — IgE-mediated mast cell degranulation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Type II hypersensitivity — anti-GBM antibody deposition

choices.label: C
choices.text: Type III hypersensitivity — circulating immune complex deposition

choices.label: D
choices.text: Type IV hypersensitivity — T cell-mediated delayed hypersensitivity

choices.label: E
choices.text: Direct bacterial invasion of the glomerulus

explanation.summary: IE-associated glomerulonephritis is caused by deposition of circulating immune complexes (antigen-antibody complexes) in the glomerular basement membrane, activating complement and causing type III hypersensitivity-mediated inflammation.
explanation.whyCorrect: In IE, continuous bacteremia generates antibodies against bacterial antigens, forming circulating immune complexes. These complexes deposit in the glomeruli (subendothelial location), activate the classical complement pathway (consuming C3 and C4, explaining low levels), and trigger inflammatory damage. This is analogous to serum sickness and lupus nephritis. The diffuse proliferative GN with subendothelial deposits and complement consumption is classic for type III hypersensitivity.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Type I hypersensitivity involves IgE-mediated immediate reactions (anaphylaxis, atopy). It does not cause glomerulonephritis.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Type II hypersensitivity involves antibodies directed against tissue antigens (e.g., anti-GBM in Goodpasture syndrome). IE-associated GN involves circulating immune complexes, not anti-GBM antibodies. Anti-GBM shows linear IgG staining, not the granular pattern seen in IC deposition.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Type IV hypersensitivity is T cell-mediated (e.g., TB granulomas, contact dermatitis). While T cells contribute to some forms of GN, the subendothelial immune complex deposits and complement consumption in this case point to type III.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Direct bacterial invasion of the glomerulus can occur (septic emboli causing renal abscesses) but would present as focal nephritis with abscess formation, not diffuse proliferative GN with immune complex deposits.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old man with recently diagnosed metastatic pancreatic adenocarcinoma presents with sudden-onset left leg weakness. MRI of the brain shows acute ischemic infarcts in multiple vascular territories. TEE reveals small, flat, sterile vegetations along the closure line of the mitral valve. Blood cultures are negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: Sterile vegetations on the mitral valve in a patient with advanced malignancy (pancreatic adenocarcinoma) and multi-territory embolic strokes is classic for nonbacterial thrombotic endocarditis (NBTE, marantic endocarditis).
explanation.whyCorrect: NBTE occurs in the setting of hypercoagulable states, particularly advanced mucin-secreting adenocarcinomas (pancreatic, lung, GI). The vegetations are composed of bland fibrin-platelet thrombi deposited along valve closure lines, without inflammatory infiltrate or bacterial organisms. They are prone to embolization, causing strokes, renal infarcts, and splenic infarcts in multiple vascular territories.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Culture-negative IE should be considered, but the sterile, flat vegetations along the closure line (not large, irregular vegetations), absence of fever or systemic infection, and the association with advanced malignancy favor NBTE.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Libman-Sacks endocarditis (SLE-associated) also produces sterile vegetations, but these characteristically occur on both surfaces of the valve (especially the undersurface) and are associated with SLE/antiphospholipid syndrome, not pancreatic cancer.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Carcinoid heart disease causes valve thickening and retraction (primarily right-sided) from serotonin-mediated fibrosis, not sterile vegetations on the mitral valve.
whyWrongByChoice.E: AF can cause cardioembolic stroke, but it would not explain the valvular vegetations seen on TEE. Additionally, AF typically causes a single embolic event rather than multi-territory infarcts.

questions.stem: A pathologist examines a mitral valve from a patient who died with disseminated mucinous adenocarcinoma. Small (1-5 mm), bland, fibrin-platelet vegetations are found along the line of closure. There is no inflammatory infiltrate, valve destruction, or bacterial organisms. How do these vegetations differ from those of acute rheumatic fever?

choices.label: A
choices.text: NBTE vegetations are larger and more destructive

choices.label: B
choices.text: NBTE vegetations lack the surrounding inflammatory Aschoff body reaction

choices.label: C
choices.text: NBTE vegetations contain bacterial colonies within the fibrin

choices.label: D
choices.text: NBTE vegetations are only found on the aortic valve

choices.label: E
choices.text: NBTE vegetations are firmly adherent and do not embolize

explanation.summary: Both NBTE and rheumatic vegetations are small and sterile, but rheumatic verrucae are associated with underlying valve inflammation (Aschoff bodies with Anitschkow cells in the myocardium, inflammatory infiltrate in the valve). NBTE vegetations are bland fibrin-platelet deposits without inflammatory reaction.
explanation.whyCorrect: NBTE vegetations consist solely of fibrin and platelets without inflammatory cells, granulomatous reaction, or tissue destruction. Rheumatic vegetations, while also small and sterile, are associated with a robust inflammatory process including Aschoff bodies, Anitschkow cells, and valvulitis. This fundamental difference in pathogenesis (hypercoagulability vs. autoimmune inflammation) is key.

whyWrongByChoice.A: NBTE vegetations are typically small (1-5 mm), similar to rheumatic verrucae. Large, destructive vegetations are characteristic of infective endocarditis, not NBTE.
whyWrongByChoice.C: NBTE vegetations are sterile by definition — they contain no bacterial organisms. The presence of bacteria would indicate infective endocarditis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: NBTE can affect any valve but most commonly involves the mitral valve (followed by the aortic valve). It is not restricted to the aortic valve.
whyWrongByChoice.E: NBTE vegetations are actually loosely adherent and highly prone to embolization, which is the main clinical concern. This is unlike rheumatic verrucae, which are firmly attached and rarely embolize.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman with systemic lupus erythematosus presents with a TIA. TEE reveals small, warty vegetations on both the atrial and ventricular surfaces of the mitral valve. She is positive for antiphospholipid antibodies. Which of the following best distinguishes her valvular lesions from classic NBTE (marantic endocarditis)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Location on both valve surfaces rather than just the closure line

choices.label: B
choices.text: Presence of bacteria within the vegetations

choices.label: D
choices.text: Vegetations larger than 3 cm in diameter

explanation.summary: Libman-Sacks endocarditis (SLE) characteristically forms vegetations on both surfaces of the valve leaflets (atrial and ventricular), especially the ventricular surface. Classic NBTE/marantic vegetations are limited to the atrial surface along the line of closure.
explanation.whyCorrect: The hallmark distinction of Libman-Sacks endocarditis is the location of vegetations on both surfaces of the valve, particularly the undersurface (ventricular side). This is unique — infective endocarditis and NBTE vegetations are typically on the atrial surface (flow side). The antiphospholipid antibodies in SLE contribute to the thrombotic tendency and may worsen valve damage.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Both Libman-Sacks and NBTE vegetations are sterile (no bacteria). The presence of bacteria would indicate infective endocarditis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Libman-Sacks endocarditis predominantly affects left-sided valves (mitral > aortic), not right-sided valves. Right-sided involvement is characteristic of carcinoid heart disease and IV drug use-associated IE.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Libman-Sacks vegetations are small (1-4 mm), similar to NBTE. Vegetations >3 cm would be exceptionally unusual for any form of endocarditis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Libman-Sacks endocarditis typically causes valve regurgitation from leaflet thickening and deformation, not stenosis. Stenosis is characteristic of rheumatic heart disease.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old woman with advanced ovarian cancer and NBTE develops recurrent arterial thromboembolism despite being on unfractionated heparin. Her platelets are 180,000/μL. Which of the following is the most appropriate anticoagulation strategy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Switch to warfarin with target INR 2.0-3.0

choices.label: C
choices.text: Add aspirin to the heparin regimen

choices.label: E
choices.text: Discontinue anticoagulation and start chemotherapy alone

explanation.summary: LMWH is the preferred anticoagulant for cancer-associated thrombosis and NBTE. It is superior to warfarin in cancer-associated hypercoagulability and provides more reliable anticoagulation than UFH.
explanation.whyCorrect: In malignancy-associated NBTE, LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin) is preferred over both UFH and warfarin. LMWH has more predictable pharmacokinetics, does not require monitoring, and has been shown to be more effective than warfarin in preventing recurrent thromboembolism in cancer patients (CLOT trial). Warfarin is less effective in cancer-associated hypercoagulability due to interactions with chemotherapy and variable nutrition.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Warfarin is less effective than LMWH for cancer-associated thrombosis. Cancer patients have unpredictable warfarin metabolism due to drug interactions, malnutrition, liver dysfunction, and variable vitamin K intake.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Adding aspirin to heparin increases bleeding risk without adequate evidence of benefit for NBTE-related arterial events. The problem is fibrin-platelet thrombi on valve surfaces, not primarily platelet aggregation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: DOACs (rivaroxaban, apixaban) have emerging evidence in cancer-associated VTE but have limited data specifically in NBTE. LMWH remains the standard of care, and some DOAC trials in high-risk cancers (particularly GI and GU) showed increased bleeding.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Discontinuing anticoagulation would leave the patient at very high risk for recurrent and potentially fatal thromboembolism. While treating the underlying cancer is important, anticoagulation must continue.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with weight loss, jaundice, and a palpable gallbladder presents with acute bilateral lower extremity ischemia. CT angiography shows saddle embolus at the aortic bifurcation. TEE reveals a mobile vegetation on the aortic valve. Blood cultures are sterile. Labs show markedly elevated CA 19-9 and a D-dimer of 8,500 ng/mL. Which of the following is the most important diagnostic step to identify the underlying cause of his valvular vegetations?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Repeat blood cultures with extended incubation for HACEK organisms

choices.label: B
choices.text: Right heart catheterization for hemodynamic assessment

choices.label: C
choices.text: ANA and anti-dsDNA antibody testing for SLE

choices.label: D
choices.text: CT abdomen with contrast to evaluate for pancreatic malignancy

explanation.summary: Painless jaundice with palpable gallbladder (Courvoisier sign), markedly elevated CA 19-9, and sterile valvular vegetations strongly suggest pancreatic head adenocarcinoma with NBTE. CT abdomen is essential to confirm the diagnosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: The clinical picture — Courvoisier sign (painless jaundice + palpable gallbladder suggesting distal bile duct obstruction), elevated CA 19-9 (pancreatic cancer marker), markedly elevated D-dimer (hypercoagulability), and sterile valve vegetations with arterial embolism — is classic for NBTE in the setting of pancreatic adenocarcinoma. CT abdomen with contrast will identify the pancreatic mass and guide further management.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While culture-negative IE from HACEK organisms should be considered, the clinical presentation (malignancy signs, hypercoagulability, sterile vegetations) overwhelmingly favors NBTE. HACEK organisms do not cause elevated CA 19-9 or Courvoisier sign.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Right heart catheterization assesses hemodynamics but does not identify the underlying cause of NBTE. The diagnostic priority is identifying the malignancy.
whyWrongByChoice.C: SLE testing would be appropriate if Libman-Sacks endocarditis were suspected, but this elderly man with obstructive jaundice and elevated tumor markers has a presentation much more consistent with malignancy-associated NBTE.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Sarcoidosis can cause cardiac involvement but does not cause NBTE, pancreatic obstruction, or elevated CA 19-9.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman with NBTE and metastatic lung adenocarcinoma develops simultaneous deep vein thrombosis in the left leg and superficial thrombophlebitis in the right arm. The superficial thrombophlebitis resolved but then appeared in a different location on her trunk. Which of the following terms best describes this pattern of thrombosis?

explanation.summary: Trousseau syndrome refers to migratory superficial thrombophlebitis (thrombophlebitis migrans) and/or recurrent DVT in the setting of occult or known malignancy. It is strongly associated with mucin-secreting adenocarcinomas and frequently co-occurs with NBTE.
explanation.whyCorrect: Trousseau syndrome encompasses the hypercoagulable manifestations of malignancy including migratory thrombophlebitis, DVT, arterial thromboembolism, and NBTE. The migratory nature (thrombophlebitis resolving in one location and appearing in another) is the hallmark. Mucin-secreting adenocarcinomas (pancreatic, lung, GI, ovarian) activate coagulation through tissue factor expression and mucin-mediated platelet aggregation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Virchow triad (endothelial injury, stasis, hypercoagulability) describes the three factors predisposing to thrombosis in general. It is a pathophysiologic concept, not a clinical syndrome describing migratory thrombophlebitis.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Phlegmasia cerulea dolens is massive iliofemoral DVT causing venous gangrene of the extremity. It is a severe form of DVT, not the migratory pattern described here.
whyWrongByChoice.C: May-Thurner syndrome is compression of the left common iliac vein by the right common iliac artery, predisposing to left leg DVT. It is anatomic, not paraneoplastic.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Budd-Chiari syndrome is hepatic vein thrombosis causing hepatic congestion and portal hypertension. While it can occur in hypercoagulable states, it does not describe the migratory thrombophlebitis pattern.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old woman with SLE and antiphospholipid syndrome has Libman-Sacks endocarditis with moderate mitral regurgitation. She has had two prior arterial thromboembolic events despite aspirin. Which of the following is the most appropriate long-term antithrombotic therapy?

explanation.summary: For recurrent arterial thromboembolism in antiphospholipid syndrome, warfarin (target INR 2.0-3.0, or higher 2.5-3.5 in some guidelines) is the standard of care. DOACs are not recommended for APS based on the TRAPS trial results.
explanation.whyCorrect: Warfarin is the anticoagulant of choice for APS with thrombotic events. The TRAPS trial (rivaroxaban vs. warfarin in triple-positive APS) was stopped early due to excess thrombotic events in the rivaroxaban arm. For patients who have failed aspirin with recurrent events, warfarin provides more effective prevention. Some experts recommend a higher INR target (2.5-3.5) for recurrent arterial events.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Aspirin monotherapy has already failed — the patient had two thromboembolic events on aspirin. Escalation to warfarin is indicated.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Rivaroxaban is contraindicated in APS, particularly triple-positive APS, based on the TRAPS trial showing increased thrombotic events compared to warfarin.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Clopidogrel provides antiplatelet activity but is insufficient for the coagulation cascade activation in APS. Warfarin is needed for adequate anticoagulation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Withholding antithrombotic therapy in a patient with APS and recurrent thromboembolism would be dangerous and contrary to all guidelines.

questions.stem: A 64-year-old man with metastatic gastric adenocarcinoma and NBTE presents with sudden-onset flank pain and hematuria. CT abdomen shows a wedge-shaped, non-enhancing area in the left kidney consistent with renal infarction. Which of the following is the mechanism of his renal infarction?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Direct tumor invasion of the renal artery

choices.label: B
choices.text: Embolization of sterile fibrin-platelet vegetations from the valve

choices.label: C
choices.text: Renal vein thrombosis from IVC compression by tumor

choices.label: D
choices.text: Disseminated intravascular coagulation with microvascular thrombosis

explanation.summary: Renal infarction in NBTE occurs from embolization of sterile fibrin-platelet vegetations from the cardiac valves to the renal arteries, causing arterial occlusion and wedge-shaped infarction.
explanation.whyCorrect: NBTE vegetations are loosely adherent and highly prone to embolization. When fragments break off, they travel through the systemic arterial circulation to end organs. The kidneys, brain, and spleen are the most commonly affected organs. The wedge-shaped, non-enhancing area is classic for arterial infarction from embolic occlusion of a segmental renal artery branch.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Direct tumor invasion of the renal artery is extremely rare with gastric cancer. Metastatic involvement more commonly affects the peritoneum or liver.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Renal vein thrombosis presents with flank pain and hematuria but produces renal swelling and hemorrhagic infarction, not the wedge-shaped arterial infarction pattern seen here.
whyWrongByChoice.D: DIC causes diffuse microvascular thrombosis with schistocytes and consumption coagulopathy. While cancer patients can develop DIC, the focal wedge-shaped infarction pattern indicates macrovascular embolism rather than diffuse microangiopathy.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Chemotherapy-induced TMA (e.g., from gemcitabine, mitomycin C) would present with diffuse microvascular injury, schistocytes, and thrombocytopenia, not a focal wedge-shaped infarction.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with cachexia and advanced colon cancer has sterile vegetations found on autopsy involving the mitral and aortic valves. The pathologist notes that the vegetations are composed entirely of fibrin and degranulated platelets, without neutrophilic infiltration or valve tissue destruction. Which of the following laboratory findings would most likely have been present during life?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Positive blood cultures with viridans streptococci

choices.label: B
choices.text: Elevated D-dimer with prolonged PT and aPTT

choices.label: C
choices.text: Positive anti-nuclear antibody with low complement

explanation.summary: NBTE in advanced malignancy is associated with chronic DIC and hypercoagulability. Elevated D-dimer (from fibrin degradation), prolonged PT/aPTT (from consumption of clotting factors), and often low-grade schistocytes reflect the underlying coagulopathy.
explanation.whyCorrect: Advanced malignancy-associated NBTE is frequently accompanied by a chronic compensated DIC state. Tumor cells express tissue factor and release procoagulant mucins, activating the coagulation cascade. This produces elevated D-dimer (fibrin degradation product), prolonged PT and aPTT (consumption of clotting factors), and sometimes thrombocytopenia. This coagulopathy drives the formation of sterile vegetations.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Positive blood cultures indicate infective endocarditis, not NBTE. By definition, NBTE vegetations are sterile.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Positive ANA with low complement suggests SLE with Libman-Sacks endocarditis, a different entity from malignancy-associated NBTE.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Elevated procalcitonin with leukocytosis indicates bacterial infection, which is inconsistent with sterile, non-inflammatory vegetations.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Rheumatoid factor may be elevated in IE (as a minor Duke criterion) but is not associated with NBTE from malignancy.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman with metastatic breast cancer has NBTE diagnosed by TEE after presenting with multi-territory cerebral infarcts. Despite therapeutic LMWH, she has a recurrent embolic stroke 2 weeks later. Repeat TEE shows persistent vegetations on the mitral valve. Which of the following is the most important prognostic factor determining her overall outcome?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Response of the underlying malignancy to treatment

explanation.summary: The prognosis of NBTE is primarily determined by the underlying malignancy. NBTE is a paraneoplastic phenomenon, and effective treatment of the cancer (if possible) is the only intervention that can resolve the hypercoagulable state driving vegetation formation.
explanation.whyCorrect: NBTE is a manifestation of the systemic hypercoagulable state caused by malignancy. While anticoagulation reduces embolic risk, it does not address the root cause. The vegetations will persist and recur as long as the underlying cancer remains active. Successful cancer treatment (chemotherapy, surgery if feasible) can resolve the coagulopathy and allow the vegetations to regress. In metastatic cancer, the prognosis is generally poor.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Vegetation size may influence embolic risk, but the overall prognosis depends on the underlying cancer, not the vegetations themselves.
whyWrongByChoice.B: MR from NBTE is usually mild to moderate and is not the primary determinant of outcome in the context of metastatic malignancy.
whyWrongByChoice.C: While LMWH is preferred over warfarin for cancer-associated thrombosis, the anticoagulant choice has a modest impact compared to the cancer prognosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: AF may increase embolic risk, but the primary driver of thromboembolism in this setting is the cancer-associated hypercoagulability, not the cardiac rhythm.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old woman presents with episodic dyspnea, dizziness, and a syncopal episode that occurred when she changed from a supine to an upright position. Auscultation reveals a low-pitched diastolic sound (tumor plop) and a diastolic rumble at the apex that changes with body position. Echocardiography shows a 4 cm pedunculated mass attached to the interatrial septum near the fossa ovalis, prolapsing through the mitral valve in diastole. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: A pedunculated mass attached to the interatrial septum at the fossa ovalis, prolapsing through the mitral valve in diastole with positional symptoms, a tumor plop, and a diastolic rumble is pathognomonic for left atrial myxoma.
explanation.whyCorrect: Myxoma is the most common primary cardiac tumor in adults. Approximately 75% arise in the left atrium, typically attached to the interatrial septum near the fossa ovalis by a stalk. Positional symptoms occur because the pedunculated tumor prolapse through the mitral valve orifice varies with gravity. The 'tumor plop' is an early diastolic sound analogous to an opening snap, produced when the tumor strikes the endocardium or obstructs the mitral valve.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Papillary fibroelastoma is the most common primary cardiac valve tumor, typically found on the aortic valve surface. It is small (<1.5 cm), avascular, and presents with embolic events rather than obstructive symptoms.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Angiosarcoma is the most common primary malignant cardiac tumor. It typically involves the right atrium and presents with hemorrhagic pericardial effusion, not a pedunculated LA mass.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Rhabdomyoma is the most common cardiac tumor in children/infants, strongly associated with tuberous sclerosis. It occurs in the ventricular myocardium, not the atrium.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Metastatic melanoma to the heart is more common than primary cardiac tumors. However, metastases are typically multiple, involve the pericardium or myocardium, and do not form pedunculated masses at the fossa ovalis.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old woman with a left atrial myxoma undergoes surgical resection. The pathology report describes a gelatinous, pedunculated mass with scattered stellate cells (lipidic myxoma cells) embedded in an acid mucopolysaccharide-rich stroma. Three years later, she develops another atrial mass on echocardiography. She also has spotty skin pigmentation and an adrenal nodule. Which of the following syndromes should be suspected?

explanation.summary: Recurrent cardiac myxoma with spotty skin pigmentation (lentigines) and an adrenal nodule (primary pigmented nodular adrenocortical disease) is the classic triad of Carney complex, an autosomal dominant syndrome caused by PRKAR1A mutations.
explanation.whyCorrect: Carney complex is an autosomal dominant syndrome characterized by cardiac myxomas (multiple, recurrent, bilateral), spotty mucocutaneous pigmentation (lentiginosis), endocrine overactivity (PPNAD causing Cushing syndrome, pituitary adenomas, thyroid tumors), and other myxomas (breast, skin). PRKAR1A gene mutations on chromosome 17q affect protein kinase A regulation. Recurrence of myxoma after resection is uncommon in sporadic cases but characteristic of Carney complex.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Tuberous sclerosis is associated with cardiac rhabdomyomas (not myxomas) in children, along with cortical tubers, subependymal giant cell astrocytomas, renal angiomyolipomas, and skin findings (ash-leaf spots, shagreen patches).
whyWrongByChoice.B: MEN1 (menin gene mutation) involves parathyroid, pituitary, and pancreatic tumors but is not associated with cardiac myxomas or skin pigmentation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Li-Fraumeni syndrome (TP53 mutation) predisposes to sarcomas, breast cancer, brain tumors, and adrenocortical carcinomas but is not associated with cardiac myxomas.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Gorlin syndrome (nevoid basal cell carcinoma syndrome) involves multiple basal cell carcinomas, jaw keratocysts, and skeletal anomalies, not cardiac myxomas.

questions.stem: A 6-month-old infant with seizures and ash-leaf spots on the skin is found to have multiple intramural cardiac masses on echocardiography. The masses are located within the ventricular myocardium and appear homogeneous and well-circumscribed. Genetic testing confirms TSC2 mutation. Which of the following statements about this cardiac tumor is correct?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The tumor requires immediate surgical excision due to malignant potential

choices.label: B
choices.text: The tumor has a high rate of distant metastasis

choices.label: C
choices.text: The tumor is composed of myxoid stromal cells

choices.label: D
choices.text: The tumor typically regresses spontaneously during childhood

choices.label: E
choices.text: The tumor is most commonly located in the left atrium

explanation.summary: Cardiac rhabdomyomas in tuberous sclerosis are hamartomatous lesions that typically regress spontaneously during childhood, often completely resolving by age 4-6. Surgery is reserved for hemodynamically significant obstruction.
explanation.whyCorrect: Rhabdomyomas are benign hamartomas composed of large 'spider cells' (glycogen-laden cardiomyocytes with central cytoplasmic mass and radiating strands). They are strongly associated with tuberous sclerosis (TSC1/TSC2 mutations). Most undergo spontaneous regression as the child grows, and conservative management with observation is appropriate unless they cause significant outflow obstruction or arrhythmias. mTOR inhibitors (everolimus) may accelerate regression.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Rhabdomyomas are benign hamartomas with no malignant potential. Surgery is only indicated if the masses cause significant hemodynamic obstruction or life-threatening arrhythmias.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Rhabdomyomas do not metastasize. They are hamartomas (disorganized growth of normal tissue), not true neoplasms. Rhabdomyosarcoma (a separate malignant tumor) can metastasize, but this is a different entity.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Myxoid stromal cells are characteristic of myxomas, not rhabdomyomas. Rhabdomyomas contain pathognomonic 'spider cells' with glycogen-rich cytoplasm.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Rhabdomyomas are located within the ventricular myocardium (most commonly the LV), not the left atrium. Left atrial location is characteristic of myxomas.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man presents with rapidly progressive dyspnea and bloody pericardial effusion. CT chest shows a large infiltrative mass in the right atrium extending into the pericardium with hemorrhagic pericardial effusion. Biopsy shows malignant spindle cells with vascular channel formation and CD31 positivity. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: An infiltrative right atrial mass with hemorrhagic pericardial effusion, malignant spindle cells forming vascular channels, and CD31 positivity (endothelial marker) is diagnostic of cardiac angiosarcoma, the most common primary malignant cardiac tumor.
explanation.whyCorrect: Cardiac angiosarcoma is the most common primary malignant cardiac tumor (~40% of primary cardiac malignancies). It has a strong predilection for the right atrium and characteristically presents with hemorrhagic pericardial effusion from tumor invasion of the pericardium. Histologically, it shows malignant endothelial cells forming irregular vascular channels. CD31, CD34, and factor VIII-related antigen are positive endothelial markers. Prognosis is poor, with median survival <1 year.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Myxoma is benign, pedunculated, found in the left atrium (75%), and does not cause hemorrhagic pericardial effusion or show malignant histology.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Cardiac rhabdomyosarcoma is a malignant tumor of striated muscle that can occur in any cardiac chamber. It would show desmin and myogenin positivity, not CD31+ vascular channels.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Primary cardiac lymphoma typically involves the right heart and can present with pericardial effusion, but histology shows monomorphic lymphoid cells (CD20+ for B-cell), not spindle cells with vascular channels.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Cardiac fibroma is a benign tumor occurring in children, usually in the ventricular septum or LV free wall. It consists of fibroblasts and collagen, not malignant spindle cells.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old woman with a history of renal cell carcinoma treated with nephrectomy 2 years ago presents with progressive dyspnea and lower extremity edema. CT abdomen and chest show a mass extending from the IVC into the right atrium. Which of the following primary tumors is most notorious for direct intracaval extension into the right heart?

explanation.summary: Renal cell carcinoma (RCC) is notorious for direct extension through the renal vein into the IVC and right atrium ('tumor thrombus'). This occurs in approximately 4-10% of RCC cases and may present with signs of right heart failure or SVC/IVC obstruction.
explanation.whyCorrect: RCC has a unique propensity for venous invasion, extending as a tumor thrombus from the renal vein into the IVC and sometimes reaching the right atrium. This intravascular growth pattern is characteristic of RCC (clear cell type). Surgical resection with IVC thrombectomy under cardiopulmonary bypass can still be curative if no distant metastases are present. Recurrence with IVC extension can occur years after initial nephrectomy.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Hepatocellular carcinoma can also invade the hepatic vein and IVC, but RCC is the classic example of intracaval extension to the right heart and is more commonly tested.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Gastric adenocarcinoma spreads by direct extension, lymphatic, and peritoneal routes but does not typically form IVC tumor thrombus.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Pancreatic adenocarcinoma invades locally (duodenum, SMA, SMV, portal vein) but does not characteristically extend through the IVC to the heart.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Colon adenocarcinoma metastasizes hematogenously to the liver (portal venous drainage) but does not typically form intracaval tumor thrombus extending to the right heart.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man is incidentally found to have a small (8 mm), mobile, papillary mass on the aortic valve surface during echocardiography performed for an unrelated reason. The mass has a characteristic 'sea anemone' appearance. He has no history of stroke or systemic embolism. Which of the following is the most appropriate management?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Immediate surgical excision regardless of symptoms

choices.label: B
choices.text: Serial echocardiographic surveillance with antiplatelet therapy

choices.label: D
choices.text: Radiation therapy to the cardiac mass

explanation.summary: A small papillary fibroelastoma on the aortic valve in an asymptomatic patient can be managed with surveillance and antiplatelet therapy. Surgical excision is recommended for large (>1 cm) or mobile masses on the left side, particularly if symptomatic with prior embolic events.
explanation.whyCorrect: Papillary fibroelastoma (PFE) is the most common primary cardiac valve tumor, typically found on the aortic valve. The 'sea anemone' appearance (papillary fronds attached by a short pedicle) is characteristic. For asymptomatic patients with small, left-sided PFE, management is debated but many experts recommend surveillance with echocardiography and antiplatelet therapy. Surgery is more clearly indicated for symptomatic patients (prior embolism), large tumors (>1 cm), or highly mobile lesions.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Immediate surgery for all PFEs is not the consensus approach. In asymptomatic patients with small tumors, the risk of surgery may outweigh the embolic risk. Guidelines support either approach (surveillance vs. surgery) for asymptomatic left-sided PFE.
whyWrongByChoice.C: PFE is a benign tumor that does not respond to chemotherapy and is not a malignancy. Chemotherapy would be inappropriate.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Radiation therapy has no role in treating benign cardiac tumors like PFE.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The 'sea anemone' appearance and smooth, regular morphology distinguish PFE from infective vegetations, which are typically irregular and associated with clinical signs of infection.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with a history of melanoma resected 3 years ago presents with new-onset atrial fibrillation and a pericardial effusion. Cardiac MRI shows multiple enhancing masses within the myocardium and pericardium. Which of the following statements about cardiac metastases is correct?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Primary cardiac tumors are more common than cardiac metastases

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cardiac metastases are best treated with surgical resection

choices.label: C
choices.text: Cardiac metastases most commonly cause obstructive valve symptoms

choices.label: D
choices.text: Melanoma has the highest rate of cardiac metastasis of any cancer

choices.label: E
choices.text: Cardiac metastases typically involve the left atrial endocardium

explanation.summary: Melanoma has the highest rate of cardiac metastasis of any malignancy — found in up to 50-65% of patients with disseminated melanoma at autopsy. Cardiac metastases are 20-40 times more common than primary cardiac tumors overall.
explanation.whyCorrect: While lung and breast cancer account for the most absolute cases of cardiac metastasis (due to their high prevalence), melanoma has the highest per-patient rate of cardiac involvement. Melanoma demonstrates remarkable tropism for the heart. Cardiac metastases are far more common than primary cardiac tumors (20-40:1 ratio), as the heart can be seeded by hematogenous spread, lymphatic spread, direct extension, or transvenous extension.

whyWrongByChoice.A: This is incorrect — cardiac metastases are 20-40 times more common than primary cardiac tumors. Primary tumors are rare (0.02% incidence).
whyWrongByChoice.B: Surgical resection is rarely indicated for cardiac metastases due to their multiplicity and the advanced stage of the underlying malignancy. Treatment is directed at the primary cancer (systemic therapy, immunotherapy for melanoma).
whyWrongByChoice.C: Cardiac metastases most commonly present with pericardial effusion (often hemorrhagic), arrhythmias, or heart failure — not obstructive valve symptoms, which are more characteristic of myxomas.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Cardiac metastases most commonly involve the pericardium and epicardial myocardium, not the endocardium. This pattern reflects the routes of metastatic spread (lymphatic and hematogenous).

questions.stem: A 48-year-old woman with a left atrial myxoma undergoes successful surgical excision. Histological analysis shows stellate cells embedded in a myxoid stroma with abundant acid mucopolysaccharide matrix. The tumor is pedunculated with a smooth, glistening surface. Which of the following is the embryologic origin of the cells composing this tumor?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Multipotent mesenchymal stem cells of the endocardium

choices.label: C
choices.text: Neural crest cells migrating to the heart

choices.label: D
choices.text: Epicardial cells from the proepicardial organ

choices.label: E
choices.text: Endothelial cells of the coronary vasculature

explanation.summary: Cardiac myxomas arise from multipotent mesenchymal stem cells (primitive mesenchymal cells) located in the subendocardium of the fossa ovalis region. This explains their typical attachment site and their ability to differentiate along multiple lineages.
explanation.whyCorrect: The stellate 'lipidic' myxoma cells are derived from primitive multipotent mesenchymal cells trapped in the subendocardium during embryologic development, particularly at the fossa ovalis (a remnant of the septum primum). These cells can produce the abundant mucopolysaccharide matrix and express both endothelial and smooth muscle markers, reflecting their mesenchymal stem cell origin.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Cardiomyocytes are the origin of rhabdomyomas and rhabdomyosarcomas, not myxomas. Myxoma cells are mesenchymal, not muscle-derived.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Neural crest cells contribute to the cardiac outflow tract, aorticopulmonary septum, and components of the cardiac conduction system, but are not the origin of myxomas.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The proepicardial organ gives rise to the epicardium, coronary smooth muscle cells, and cardiac fibroblasts, not to intracavitary tumors like myxomas.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Coronary endothelial cells do not give rise to myxomas. Endothelial-derived tumors include hemangiomas and angiosarcomas, which have different histologic features.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman with a left atrial myxoma presents with fever, weight loss, elevated ESR, elevated IL-6 levels, anemia, and hypergammaglobulinemia. These constitutional symptoms resolve completely after surgical excision of the tumor. Which of the following best explains the constitutional symptoms associated with cardiac myxoma?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Secondary infection of the myxoma with bacterial colonization

choices.label: C
choices.text: Paraneoplastic antibody production against cardiac antigens

choices.label: D
choices.text: Chronic hemolysis from turbulent flow around the tumor

choices.label: E
choices.text: Production of interleukin-6 and other cytokines by the tumor

explanation.summary: Cardiac myxomas produce interleukin-6 (IL-6) and other cytokines, causing a systemic inflammatory syndrome that mimics autoimmune disease or endocarditis (fever, elevated ESR/CRP, anemia, weight loss, hypergammaglobulinemia). These symptoms resolve after tumor resection.
explanation.whyCorrect: Myxoma cells constitutively produce IL-6, a proinflammatory cytokine that drives hepatic acute phase protein production (elevated ESR, CRP), B-cell activation (hypergammaglobulinemia), and systemic inflammation (fever, weight loss, anemia of chronic disease). This 'myxoma syndrome' can mimic endocarditis, vasculitis, or connective tissue disease, leading to diagnostic delay. Complete resolution after excision confirms the tumor as the cytokine source.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Myxomas are not infected tumors. The sterile inflammatory response is driven by tumor-produced cytokines, not bacterial colonization. Blood cultures are negative.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Renal embolism from myxoma fragments can occur but would present with flank pain and hematuria, not the systemic inflammatory syndrome described.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Paraneoplastic antibodies are not the mechanism. The constitutional symptoms are directly caused by IL-6 and other cytokines produced by the myxoma cells themselves.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Chronic hemolysis can occur from mechanical red cell damage by the tumor, but it does not explain the fever, elevated ESR, or hypergammaglobulinemia pattern seen in this patient.

questions.stem: A 10-year-old boy with a cardiac fibroma in the interventricular septum develops recurrent ventricular tachycardia refractory to antiarrhythmic medications. The tumor is 5 cm and involves the conduction system. Cardiac MRI shows a homogeneous, well-circumscribed intramural mass with low signal on T2 and late gadolinium enhancement. Surgical resection is deemed too risky due to conduction system involvement. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Observation with repeat imaging in 6 months

choices.label: C
choices.text: Radiofrequency catheter ablation of all ventricular tissue

choices.label: D
choices.text: External beam radiation to the cardiac mass

explanation.summary: Cardiac fibroma with refractory ventricular arrhythmias that cannot be surgically resected due to conduction system involvement is one of the indications for cardiac transplantation in pediatric patients. Cardiac fibromas do not respond to chemotherapy or radiation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Cardiac fibromas are benign but can be life-threatening due to refractory arrhythmias (VT/VF) or hemodynamic obstruction, particularly when involving the interventricular septum and conduction system. When the tumor is unresectable and arrhythmias are refractory to medical management and ablation, cardiac transplantation may be the only definitive option. Fibromas do not regress spontaneously (unlike rhabdomyomas) and do not respond to chemotherapy.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Observation is inappropriate for refractory VT, which poses an immediate risk of sudden cardiac death. Active intervention is needed.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Cardiac fibromas are benign tumors composed of fibroblasts and collagen. They do not respond to chemotherapy, which is reserved for malignant cardiac tumors.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Catheter ablation may be attempted for arrhythmia control, but ablating 'all ventricular tissue' is not feasible. Ablation of the arrhythmia circuit may provide temporary control but often fails when the substrate is a large intramural tumor.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Radiation therapy is not effective for benign cardiac fibromas and carries risks of radiation-induced cardiac damage, pericarditis, and coronary artery disease.
